column1:
SYSTEM:
You are a clinical documentation specialist for internal medicine in Nova Scotia, Canada. Current date is: {CURRENT_DATE}. You will receive a long mixed-quality bundle (transcripts, referral letters, prior notes, labs, imaging, ECGs, portal text). First, read the entire bundle, then internally clean and normalize it before writing: remove non-clinical filler and portal/UI noise (e.g., "Click here...", "other clinicians who viewed...", headers/footers, routing metadata, duplicated blocks). De-duplicate repeated content and keep the most complete copy. Identity and consistency: preserve patient name/DOB/sex/PHN exactly as provided and keep them consistent; never guess missing identifiers; do not change age unless DOB is provided and age is logically derivable. Temporal provenance: extract all explicit dates/times; organize facts chronologically; clearly separate current-encounter information from prior records; never present prior data as current; never create future dates beyond {CURRENT_DATE}; when clinically relevant, include the date or relative timing (e.g., prior, earlier this year) for key facts in History of Present Illness, Investigations, and Impression; do not collapse information from different dates into a single undated statement; if the current encounter date is not explicit, use {CURRENT_DATE} only as an ordering anchor and do not claim events occurred today unless explicitly stated. Conflict handling: if sources disagree, do not silently resolve; state the conflict and attribute each to its dated source. Physical exam (encounter-bound): include exam findings only if explicitly documented as performed in the current encounter; if exam is explicitly deferred, write "Physical exam: deferred" and do not include any exam findings from prior encounters even if present; if telephone/virtual with no exam, write "Physical exam: not performed"; if absent with no indication, write "Physical exam: not documented". Vitals: include vitals only if explicitly documented for the current encounter; do not infer, estimate, or carry forward prior vitals. Allergies: list exactly as documented; if not present, write "Allergies: not documented"; never assume NKDA. Medications: the most recent dated medication list in the input is the authoritative current list; do not invent drugs, doses, routes, or frequencies; use generic names only; format each medication as "Drug dose unit route frequency" (spell out route and frequency), one per line; medication name integrity: if another part of the bundle mentions a medication that does not clearly match the authoritative list and cannot be safely normalized, do not guess—list it under "Medication uncertainties:" at the end with "[NEED MORE INFO: exact drug name/dose]"; use Medication uncertainties only when necessary and re-check the source if more than 3 items appear there. Devices/implants/proper nouns: for prosthetic valves, pacemakers, stents, implants, manufacturers, model numbers, and sizes, preserve exact wording from the source; do not paraphrase or substitute similar-sounding names; if unclear, flag "[VERIFY: device/implant specification]" rather than guessing. Investigations: in the Investigations section, list only relevant tests with dates and numeric results/units exactly as provided; do not include reference ranges; do not label results as high/low/abnormal; do not interpret in that section (interpretation and severity emphasis belong in Impression). Numeric plausibility safeguard: do not correct values, but if a value-unit pair is physiologically implausible or magnitude seems inconsistent with typical units, flag "[VERIFY: implausible value/unit for X - check source]". False reassurance guard: do not write "ruled out", "no evidence of", or definitive exclusions unless the source uses equivalent definitive language; when a negative test does not definitively exclude an important diagnosis and that limitation is clinically relevant, acknowledge the limitation in Impression using cautious language. Impression requirements: begin with the primary problem or most clinically important diagnosis driving management, followed by secondary active problems in descending clinical importance; avoid long equal-weight lists; emphasize critical red flags and high-risk findings in Impression using appropriate clinical language derived from provided data. Plan authority and safety addendum: if the current encounter includes an explicit clinician plan or next step (including narrative statements), it must be reflected as the primary Plan and must not be overridden by older plans or generic best practice; before finalizing Plan, check for internal conflicts (mutually incompatible recommendations) and if found, flag "[CONFLICT IN PLAN: ...]" rather than listing both; Addendum (safety) may be used only for clearly justified safety gaps based on current-encounter data and limited to life-threatening omissions, critical allergy or interaction conflicts, or mandatory safety monitoring for explicitly documented high-risk therapies; do not add generic best practices or alternative pathways. Severe infection specificity: if antibiotics are documented, name them explicitly; if severe infection or sepsis is clearly present but antibiotics are not documented, do not invent—flag "[NEED MORE INFO: antibiotics selected]". Urgency wording: specify timing (emergent, urgent, routine) only if explicitly stated in the source or if a time-critical syndrome is clearly supported by current-encounter data; otherwise do not invent timing and use "[NEED MORE INFO: timing]" if required. Responsibility attribution: do not assign responsibility implicitly (e.g., "we will monitor", "team will follow") unless explicitly stated in the source; when responsibility is unclear, use neutral phrasing. Output formatting: plain text only (no markdown, no tables, no separators). Section titles are simple words ending with a colon on their own line. Bullets are allowed. Use numerals not words. Vitals format if present: BP 132/60 mmHg, HR 75 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 98% RA. FINAL OUTPUT CONTROL: you may think freely, but the final note saved by the app must appear only between the markers "FINAL NOTE START" and "FINAL NOTE END"; do not include anything outside those markers in the final note.

USER:
Write a consult note answering the referral question for the current encounter. Focus on the active problem(s) relevant to this referral and today’s safety or management; exclude chronic conditions unless they directly affect today’s assessment, risk stratification, medication choice, or disposition. Do not copy forward long chronic problem lists from old notes. Use this section order: Patient ID, History of Present Illness, Past Medical History, Medications, Allergies, Family and Social History, Physical exam, Investigations, Impression, Plan. In Impression, start with the primary problem driving management and then list secondary active issues in descending importance with brief rationale. In Plan, reflect the clinician’s stated plan if present; include specific actionable steps supported by current data, and assign responsibility only when explicitly stated in the source.

FINAL NOTE START  
Patient ID: Karim Shaloh, 42 years  

Subjective:  
The patient reports new onset severe chest pain prompting presentation to the emergency department. He denies any prior medical conditions or similar episodes previously. DR: MBAH, CHIKAODINAKA S MD FAMILY PHYSICIAN: GILLIS,JOSEPH M MD COPIES TO: GILLIS,JOSEPH M MD; MBAH,CHIKAODINAKA S MD CATEGORIE(S): MAGNETIC RESONANCE IMAGING PROCEDURE(S): LSPINE WWO WITH 6PULVC MRI of lumbar spine (enhanced) Indication: Fever for 5 days. Blood in urine culture was positive for Staphylococcus aureus. WBC 29, CRP 298. Acute back pain. Discitis, osteomyelitis or abscess? Findings: Comparison is made to multiple prior studies, including CT 11/11/2021. Extremely limited examination due to significant motion artifact in this patient who has constant twitching and shaking in his legs. Allowing for this limitation, AP alignment of the lumbar spine appears within normal limits. Vertebral body heights and disc spaces are maintained. Conus appears to terminate normally posterior to L1. No definite signal abnormality in the visualized cord. No definite marrow signal abnormality nor abnormal signal in the visualized disc spaces to suggest discitis or osteomyelitis. No discrete fluid collection or abscess is identified. Postcontrast sequences are limited by significant motion artifact. No definite abnormal enhancement is identified. There is multilevel degenerative disc disease. There are mild annular bulges at L12, L23 and L5S1 that do not appear to cause significant stenosis. Moderate bilateral hypertrophic facet joint osteoarthritis at L45 and L5S1 with associated bilateral facet joint effusions. No convincing spinal canal stenosis or foraminal narrowing. Vaguely wedgeshaped regions of hypoenhancement partly imaged in the lower pole of the right kidney and also the upper pole of the left kidney. Impression: Limited examination with significant motion artifact does not demonstrate any convincing evidence of spondylodiscitis, osteomyelitis or paravertebral or other abscess. Mild to moderate multilevel degenerative changes with facet joint osteoarthritis and effusions in the lower lumbar spine. Partly imaged kidneys demonstrate bilateral somewhat wedgeshaped regions of decreased perfusion. In this clinical context, the appearance raises the possibility of pyelonephritis. FINAL NOTE START  
Patient ID: Arthur Donald Belliveau, DOB 29/10/1963, PHN 0008882730  

Chief Complaint: Fever, altered mental status, hypotension requiring ICU transfer.  

History of Present Illness:  
On 26 Oct 2025 the patient noted rising temperature (up to 38 °C) and became increasingly disoriented. Vital sign trends were not recorded in this encounter, but the attending physician reports “BP is becoming soft” and the patient is “very confused.” A blood culture drawn 26 Oct 2025 yielded *Staphylococcus aureus* (reported 20:35 hrs). Subsequent cultures collected later that evening remained negative. The patient has been admitted to the hospital for several days prior to this episode. Over the past week laboratory data demonstrate persistent leukocytosis (WBC 11–12 × 10⁹/L), markedly elevated C‑reactive protein (>200 mg/L), and a modest rise in serum creatinine (≈80 µmol/L). Electrolyte abnormalities include hyponatremia (Na = 134–142 mmol/L) and hypokalemia (K = 3.2–3.6 mmol/L). No imaging or physical exam was performed during this admission; all relevant investigations are listed below.  

Relevant Past Medical History:  
• Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 40%) secondary to dilated cardiomyopathy and severe aortic regurgitation; bioprosthetic Edwards Lifesciences 27 mm CE Perimount Magna Ease aortic valve implanted Aug 24 2020.  
• Hypertension, dyslipidemia, former smoker (60 pack‑years, quit 11 yrs ago); current marijuana vaping 3 g/day.  
• Alcohol use disorder – abstinent ≥12 yrs.  
• Asthma (history of bronchial asthma).  
• Chronic right chest, neck, and spinal pain post‑trauma (14 yrs ago).  

Medications: Not documented in the most recent dated medication list.  

Allergies: No known allergies.  

Family and Social History: Lives alone with his dog; receives disability benefits under workers’ compensation for traumatic injury.  

Physical Examination: Not performed during this admission; therefore “Physical exam: not performed”.  

Investigations:  
Laboratory (most recent dates):  
— 26 Oct 2025: CBC: WBC 11.1 x10⁹/L, Hb 133 g/L, Plt 12 x10⁶/L; Creatinine 79 µmol/L; Na 136 mmol/L; K 3.2 mmol/L; CRP 298 mg/L.  
— 28 Oct 2025: CBC: WBC 20.0 x10⁹/L, Cr 72 µmol/L; CRP 225 mg/L; Na 142 mmol/L; K 3.5 mmol/L.

DR: MBAH, CHIKAODINAKA S MD FAMILY PHYSICIAN: GILLIS,JOSEPH M MD COPIES TO: GILLIS,JOSEPH M MD; MBAH,CHIKAODINAKA S MD CATEGORIE(S): MAGNETIC RESONANCE IMAGING PROCEDURE(S): LSPINE WWO WITH 6PULVC MRI of lumbar spine (enhanced) Indication: Fever for 5 days. Blood in urine culture was positive for Staphylococcus aureus. WBC 29, CRP 298. Acute back pain. Discitis, osteomyelitis or abscess? Findings: Comparison is made to multiple prior studies, including CT 11/11/2021. Extremely limited examination due to significant motion artifact in this patient who has constant twitching and shaking in his legs. Allowing for this limitation, AP alignment of the lumbar spine appears within normal limits. Vertebral body heights and disc spaces are maintained. Conus appears to terminate normally posterior to L1. No definite signal abnormality in the visualized cord. No definite marrow signal abnormality nor abnormal signal in the visualized disc spaces to suggest discitis or osteomyelitis. No discrete fluid collection or abscess is identified. Postcontrast sequences are limited by significant motion artifact. No definite abnormal enhancement is identified. There is multilevel degenerative disc disease. There are mild annular bulges at L12, L23 and L5S1 that do not appear to cause significant stenosis. Moderate bilateral hypertrophic facet joint osteoarthritis at L45 and L5S1 with associated bilateral facet joint effusions. No convincing spinal canal stenosis or foraminal narrowing. Vaguely wedgeshaped regions of hypoenhancement partly imaged in the lower pole of the right kidney and also the upper pole of the left kidney. Impression: Limited examination with significant motion artifact does not demonstrate any convincing evidence of spondylodiscitis, osteomyelitis or paravertebral or other abscess. Mild to moderate multilevel degenerative changes with facet joint osteoarthritis and effusions in the lower lumbar spine. Partly imaged kidneys demonstrate bilateral somewhat wedgeshaped regions of decreased perfusion. In this clinical context, the appearance raises the possibility of pyelonephritis. FINAL NOTE START  
**Patient ID:**  
Name: Arthur Donald Belliveau  
DOB: 29/10/1963  
Sex: Male  
Health Card Number: 0008882730  

**Subjective:**  
Chief Complaint: “Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia” with confusion and worsening blood pressure (“BP is becoming soft”).  
History of Present Illness: The patient was admitted for several days following an aortic valve replacement in 2020. Over the past few days his mental status deteriorated and vital signs have become unstable. He reports feeling disoriented and unable to maintain adequate blood pressure despite therapy. Past medical history significant for severe aortic regurgitation repaired with a 27 mm Edwards Lifesciences Perimount Magna Ease bioprosthesis, congestive heart failure with LVEF 40% pre‑operatively, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, former heavy smoking, marijuana vaping, bronchial asthma, chronic right chest/spinal pain from trauma, alcohol overuse disorder (now abstinent), and type II diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 6.5).  

**Objective:**  
*Current Encounter:* Physical examination data are **not documented**; therefore “Physical exam: not performed.”  
Investigations (most recent):  
• CBC (10/28/25): WBC 11.1 × 10⁹/L, Hb 133 g/L, Plt 12 × 10⁹/L, Neutrophils ↑, Platelets ↓.  
• Serum chemistries (10/28/25): Na 142 mEq/L, K 3.5 mEq/L, Cl 112 mEq/L, Cr 72 µmol/L, BUN 7.2 mmol/L, Albumin 25 g/L, AST 40 U/L, ALT 69 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 285 U/L, Total bilirubin 25.3 µmol/L, Direct bilirubin 19.2 µmol/L.  
• C‑reactive protein (10/28/25): 225 mg/L.  
• Electrolyte panel (10/28/25) repeated same values.  
• Urinalysis (10/26/25): Positive nitrites, leukocyte esterase high, WBC 510/hpf, RBC 2550/hpf, trace hematuria.  
• Chest radiograph (10/26/25): Clear lung fields, no consolidation or effusion.  
• ECG (10/26/25): Sinus tachycardia 116 bpm, premature atrial complexes, bifascicular block, QTc 503 ms.  
• Transthoracic echo (10/28/25): Normal LV cavity, estimated EF ~65%; mild RV dilation; mean transprosthetic gradient 23.4 mmHg, peak 35.3 mmHg; no vegetation seen.  
Blood cultures collected 10/26/25 reported *negative*. However narrative indicates ongoing suspicion of Staphylococcus aureus bacteraemia.  

**Assessment:**  
1. Suspected sepsis / septic shock likely secondary to Staphylococcus aureus infection (conflict: cultures negative vs clinical suspicion).  
2. Prosthetic aortic valve with elevated gradients—possible early dysfunction or subclinical endocarditis.  
3. Acute kidney function remains within baseline (Cr 72 µmol/L); monitoring required given sepsis risk.  
4. Anemia and thrombocytopenia consistent with consumptive process/sepsis.  
5. Hyperglycaemia (POC glucose 7.0–8.5 mmol/L) requiring glycaemic control.  
6. Gallstone disease noted on abdominal ultrasound; currently asymptomatic.  

**Plan:**  
- Immediate transfer to intensive care unit for close hemodynamic monitoring and management of suspected septic shock.  
- Initiate empiric broad‑spectrum intravenous antibiotics covering MRSA: vancomycin plus cefepime (dosage adjusted per renal function).  
- Obtain repeat blood cultures before antibiotic initiation; send for culture sensitivity.  
- Monitor serial lactates, complete metabolic panels, coagulation profile every 24 h.  
- Consult Infectious Disease for antimicrobial stewardship and potential de-escalation once sensitivities available.  
- Cardiology consult for prosthetic valve evaluation; consider transesophageal echocardiogram to rule out vegetative lesions.  
- Fluid resuscitation guided by dynamic parameters; start vasopressors if MAP <65 mmHg after fluid challenge.  
- Continue diuretic therapy as needed based on volume status; monitor electrolytes closely.  
- Insulin sliding scale protocol for hyperglycemia; target capillary glucose 7.8–10.0 mmol/L.  
- Maintain anticoagulation status – INR currently 1.0; reassess necessity given prosthetic valve and sepsis.  
- Provide airway protection due to altered mental status; consider intubation if respiratory compromise develops.  
- Daily review of laboratory trends; adjust medication doses accordingly.  
- Educate family regarding ICU course and obtain consent for advanced directives if necessary.  

FINAL NOTE END

FINAL NOTE START  
Patient ID: Romans, Bruce Frederick – DOB 24/08/1943 – Sex M – PHN 0003818101  

History of Present Illness:  
82‑year‑old male presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea since an episode of pneumonia several months ago. Reports becoming short of breath after walking approximately 12 blocks and requiring about 35 minutes of rest before resuming activity. No chest pain, orthopnoea, diaphoresis, light‑headedness, or leg swelling noted. Occasional “gas” abdominal discomfort and nighttime cough with sensation of difficulty breathing reported. He is currently using Symbicort 200 µg twice daily plus frequent use of albuterol (≈34 doses per day). Past history of COPD exacerbation post‑pneumonia and persistent neutrophilia. Recent Holter monitoring revealed two brief episodes of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia (longest 14 beats), otherwise normal sinus rhythm.  

Past Medical History (active problems):  
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) – reactive airway disease confirmed by PFTs Dec 2024.  
- Hypertension – well‑controlled on amlodipine 10 mg QD.  
- Dyslipidaemia – managed with atorvastatin 10 mg QD.  
- Anemia of chronic inflammation – ferritin high (840 ug/L), low iron/TIBC, mild normocytic changes.  
- Lyme disease – treated August 2024.  
- Sigmoid diverticulitis with adjacent loculated abscess – prior imaging.  
- Osteoarthritis of shoulders/hands – radiographic degenerative changes.  
- Mild atherosclerotic vascular disease – carotid Doppler negative for significant stenosis.  
- Chronic kidney function eGFR ~70 mL/min/1.73 m².  

Medications (most recent dated list):  
ACHROSUAVASTATIN 10 mg oral once daily  
AMLODIPINE 10 mg oral once daily  
OMEPRAZOLE 40 mg oral once daily  
APODOXY 100 mg capsule BID (generic azithromycin?)  
APONAPROXEN 500 mg tablet BID (naproxen)  
APOSALBUTAMOL HFA 100 mcg inhalation 2 puffs PRN every 4 hours  
CLAVULIN 875/125 mg tablet BID (clavulanic acid + amoxicillin)  
COLCHICINE 0.6 mg tablet Day 1: take 2 tablets then 1 one hour later; thereafter 0.6 mg PO once daily until symptom resolution  
SYMBICORT 200 µg inhalation 2 puffs BID  
VIAGRA 100 mg tablet 0.51 tablet once daily PRN  
GABAPENTIN 300 mg capsule BID PRN  
POLYETHYLENE GLYCEROL 3350 g PO 17 g/day PRN  

Allergies: None documented.  

Family & Social History: Retired driver, works as HOPE van operator. Former heavy smoker (45 pack‑years), quit several years ago but now smokes occasionally (~10 cigarettes/day). Lives independently; social isolation not described.  

Physical Exam: Not fully documented during this encounter; the provider performed auscultation noting clear lungs bilaterally and distant heart sounds consistent with hyperinflation.  

Investigations:  
PFTs (Dec 03 2024): FEV₁ 98% predicted, FVC 114%, FEV₁/FVC 64%; 13% improvement post‑bronchodilator → reactive airway disease.  
CBC (Aug 29 2025): WBC 11.3 ×10⁹/L (↑), Neutrophils 9.6 ×10⁹/L, Eosinophils 1.1 ×10⁹/L, Hb 136 g/L, Hct 0.404 L/L, ESR 65 mm/Hr, CRP 40.3 mg/L.  
Troponin T (Mar 21 2025): 18 ng/L (slightly above reference ≤14 ng/L).  
ECG (Mar 21 2025): Normal sinus rhythm, no ST/T abnormalities.  
Holter (Apr 22 2025): Two non‑sustained VT runs (max 14 beats); otherwise normal rhythm.  
Chest X‑ray (Feb 13 2024): Vague patchy opacity at left base suggestive of evolving pneumonia; otherwise stable.  
CT abdomen/pelvis (Oct 03 2024): Severe sigmoid diverticulosis without active inflammation; small bilateral renal cortical cysts; severe lumbar disc degeneration.  
Echocardiogram: Pending results from referral.  

Impression:  
Primary problem driving management: Exacerbated COPD causing marked exertional dyspnea likely secondary to ongoing airway inflammation and possible superimposed infection.  
Secondary issues:  
• Non‑sustained ventricular tachycardia observed on Holter—requires cardiology follow‑up but no evidence of acute ischemia or heart failure.  
• Persistent anemia/inflammation evidenced by elevated ferritin, ESR, CRP—may contribute to fatigue and exercise intolerance.  
• Ongoing smoking habit increases risk of respiratory decline and cardiovascular events.  

Plan:  
1. Continue current bronchodilator regimen; discuss transition to a LABA+LAMA combination (e.g., tiotropium add‑on) if symptoms persist after next review.  
2. Review inhaler technique and adherence; reinforce use of albuterol only as rescue.  
3. Arrange cardiology evaluation for Holter findings and consider repeat ECG/Echo when available.  
4. Initiate structured smoking cessation program (nicotine replacement therapy or bupropion/nortriptyline per patient preference).  
5. Consider short course antibiotics if clinical signs of bacterial pneumonia develop (fever, purulent sputum, rising inflammatory markers).  
6. Schedule pulmonary rehabilitation assessment in community setting; coordinate with local PT services.  
7. Repeat CBC, iron studies, and inflammatory markers in 8 weeks to assess trend.  
8. Maintain antihypertensive and lipid control; continue statin unless contraindicated.  
9. Ensure vaccinations up-to-date (influenza, pneumococcal PPSV23/PCV20).  
10. Follow-up appointment in 4 weeks or sooner if worsening dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, or new cardiac symptoms.

FINAL NOTE END

CATEGORIE(S): CAT SCAN
PROCEDURE(S): ABD PELVIS WITH CONTRASTAB
CT abdomen pelvis (enhanced)
Indication: Progressive unintentional weight loss. I are deficient seen. Rule
out signs of
cancer in the abdomen pelvis.
Findings:
Comparison is made to multiple prior studies, including CT 13/11/2020.
Significant motion artifact limited the initial attempt, which was repeated.
There remains
some motion artifact. There is a lack of abdominal fat. Surgical clips are
noted in the
right groin presumably related to remote hernia repair.
Allowing for this, no concerning focal liver lesion. No biliary ductal
dilation. Gallbladder
is unremarkable. Spleen is not enlarged. Pancreas and adrenal glands are
within normal
limits. Small hiatal hernia. Stomach is otherwise unremarkable. Small and
large bowel are
normal in caliber without evidence of obstruction. Severe sigmoid
diverticulosis without
definite active pericolonic inflammation.
Prostate gland is moderately enlarged. Urinary bladder is unremarkable.
Kidneys demonstrate
small bilateral renal cortical cysts. No hydroureteronephrosis.
Severe atherosclerotic disease. Mild ectasia of the infrarenal abdominal
aorta. No aneurysm.
No enlarged lymph nodes by size criteria.
Severe degenerative disc disease in the lumbar spine. No aggressive appearing
osseous
lesion.
Impression:
Limited study. No definite features of malignancy. There is a relative lack
of abdominal fat
compatible with the history which makes interpretation challenging. Severe
sigmoid
diverticulosis with some associated mural thickening but no definite
inflammatory stranding.
A similar appearance is been present on multiple prior studies; has this been
investigated
endoscopically?

Clinical history: Assess for AVN of the left hip. Severe left leg pain with
radiculopathy
to the left shin.
Comparison: CT abdomen/pelvis from November 13, 2020.
Findings:
Lumbar spine
No compression fracture is seen. Mild convex right curvature in the lumbar
spine similar to
prior. Mild retrolisthesis of L3 on L4 similar to prior. Alignment is
otherwise
maintained. There is mild disc space narrowing at L23 and severe disc space
narrowing at
L34 consistent with degenerative disc disease.
Sacroiliac joints
Mild degenerative changes noted at the sacroiliac joints. No bony erosion is
seen to
suggest sacroiliitis.
Pelvis/left hip
The bony pelvis appears intact. There is mild joint space narrowing
involving the left hip
consistent with mild degenerative change. No abnormal density or shape
involving the left
femoral head to suggest AVM.

FINAL NOTE START  
Patient ID: Romans, Bruce Frederick – DOB 24/08/1943 – Sex M – PHN 0003818101  

History of Present Illness:  
82‑year‑old male presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea since an episode of pneumonia several months ago. Reports becoming short of breath after walking approximately 12 blocks and requiring about 35 minutes of rest before resuming activity. No chest pain, orthopnoea, diaphoresis, light‑headedness, or leg swelling noted. Occasional “gas” abdominal discomfort and nighttime cough with sensation of difficulty breathing reported. He is currently using Symbicort 200 µg twice daily plus frequent use of albuterol (≈34 doses per day). Past history of COPD exacerbation post‑pneumonia and persistent neutrophilia. Recent Holter monitoring revealed two brief episodes of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia (longest 14 beats), otherwise normal sinus rhythm.  

Past Medical History (active problems):  
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) – reactive airway disease confirmed by PFTs Dec 2024.  
- Hypertension – well‑controlled on amlodipine 10 mg QD.  
- Dyslipidaemia – managed with atorvastatin 10 mg QD.  
- Anemia of chronic inflammation – ferritin high (840 ug/L), low iron/TIBC, mild normocytic changes.  
- Lyme disease – treated August 2024.  
- Sigmoid diverticulitis with adjacent loculated abscess – prior imaging.  
- Osteoarthritis of shoulders/hands – radiographic degenerative changes.  
- Mild atherosclerotic vascular disease – carotid Doppler negative for significant stenosis.  
- Chronic kidney function eGFR ~70 mL/min/1.73 m².  

Medications (most recent dated list):  
ACHROSUAVASTATIN 10 mg oral once daily  
AMLODIPINE 10 mg oral once daily  
OMEPRAZOLE 40 mg oral once daily  
APODOXY 100 mg capsule BID (generic azithromycin?)  
APONAPROXEN 500 mg tablet BID (naproxen)  
APOSALBUTAMOL HFA 100 mcg inhalation 2 puffs PRN every 4 hours  
CLAVULIN 875/125 mg tablet BID (clavulanic acid + amoxicillin)  
COLCHICINE 0.6 mg tablet Day 1: take 2 tablets then 1 one hour later; thereafter 0.6 mg PO once daily until symptom resolution  
SYMBICORT 200 µg inhalation 2 puffs BID  
VIAGRA 100 mg tablet 0.51 tablet once daily PRN  
GABAPENTIN 300 mg capsule BID PRN  
POLYETHYLENE GLYCEROL 3350 g PO 17 g/day PRN  

Allergies: None documented.  

Family & Social History: Retired driver, works as HOPE van operator. Former heavy smoker (45 pack‑years), quit several years ago but now smokes occasionally (~10 cigarettes/day). Lives independently; social isolation not described.  

Physical Exam: Not fully documented during this encounter; the provider performed auscultation noting clear lungs bilaterally and distant heart sounds consistent with hyperinflation.  

Investigations:  
PFTs (Dec 03 2024): FEV₁ 98% predicted, FVC 114%, FEV₁/FVC 64%; 13% improvement post‑bronchodilator → reactive airway disease.  
CBC (Aug 29 2025): WBC 11.3 ×10⁹/L (↑), Neutrophils 9.6 ×10⁹/L, Eosinophils 1.1 ×10⁹/L, Hb 136 g/L, Hct 0.404 L/L, ESR 65 mm/Hr, CRP 40.3 mg/L.  
Troponin T (Mar 21 2025): 18 ng/L (slightly above reference ≤14 ng/L).  
ECG (Mar 21 2025): Normal sinus rhythm, no ST/T abnormalities.  
Holter (Apr 22 2025): Two non‑sustained VT runs (max 14 beats); otherwise normal rhythm.  
Chest X‑ray (Feb 13 2024): Vague patchy opacity at left base suggestive of evolving pneumonia; otherwise stable.  
CT abdomen/pelvis (Oct 03 2024): Severe sigmoid diverticulosis without active inflammation; small bilateral renal cortical cysts; severe lumbar disc degeneration.  
Echocardiogram: Pending results from referral.  

Impression:  
Primary problem driving management: Exacerbated COPD causing marked exertional dyspnea likely secondary to ongoing airway inflammation and possible superimposed infection.  
Secondary issues:  
• Non‑sustained ventricular tachycardia observed on Holter—requires cardiology follow‑up but no evidence of acute ischemia or heart failure.  
• Persistent anemia/inflammation evidenced by elevated ferritin, ESR, CRP—may contribute to fatigue and exercise intolerance.  
• Ongoing smoking habit increases risk of respiratory decline and cardiovascular events.  

Plan:  
1. Continue current bronchodilator regimen; discuss transition to a LABA+LAMA combination (e.g., tiotropium add‑on) if symptoms persist after next review.  
2. Review inhaler technique and adherence; reinforce use of albuterol only as rescue.  
3. Arrange cardiology evaluation for Holter findings and consider repeat ECG/Echo when available.  
4. Initiate structured smoking cessation program (nicotine replacement therapy or bupropion/nortriptyline per patient preference).  
5. Consider short course antibiotics if clinical signs of bacterial pneumonia develop (fever, purulent sputum, rising inflammatory markers).  
6. Schedule pulmonary rehabilitation assessment in community setting; coordinate with local PT services.  
7. Repeat CBC, iron studies, and inflammatory markers in 8 weeks to assess trend.  
8. Maintain antihypertensive and lipid control; continue statin unless contraindicated.  
9. Ensure vaccinations up-to-date (influenza, pneumococcal PPSV23/PCV20).  
10. Follow-up appointment in 4 weeks or sooner if worsening dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, or new cardiac symptoms.

FINAL NOTE END

Patient ID: DENTREMONT, VAUGHN VICTOR | DOB: 18/01/1958 | Sex: Male | PHN: 0002637148

Subjective:
• Reports feeling well overall.
• Ongoing fatigue noted; does not experience naps frequently.
• Denies night sweats, chills, fever, recent infections, bleeding, bruising, hematuria, melena.
• Denies shortness of breath, cough; produces some clear morning sputum.
• Ex‑smoker, 35 pack‑years; quit smoking during this visit.
• Has been attending cardiovascular clinic since last Monday.
• Confirms complete abstinence from alcohol (“zero”).
• Mentions platelets are improving and potassium now within normal limits.
• Expresses satisfaction that stress test was “okay”.

Objective:
Vital Signs (10/23/25):
BP 125/66 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, RR not recorded, Temp not recorded, SpO₂ 98% RA.

Physical Exam (10/23/25):
Chest – clear lung fields bilaterally, adequate air entry.
Cardiovascular – normal S₁ and S₂, no additional sounds or murmurs.
Neurologic – unremarkable.
No jugular venous distention, no lower limb edema.

Investigations:
Stress Test – Bruce ramp protocol, collected 10/23/25 10:30 AM. Max heart rate 109 BPM, max BP 150/74 mmHg, total exercise time 0:05:26, workload 9.1 METS. Findings: NSR with nonspecific ST/T changes, isolated ventricular premature beats, no chest pain or pathologic ST changes. Result: Nondiagnostic but reassuring. Confirmed by Dr. Brian Moses.

Complete Blood Count – 10/23/25 08:57 AM. WBC 6.5 ×10⁹/L, RBC 4.10 ×10¹²/L, Hemoglobin 125 g/L, Hematocrit 0.379 L/L, MCV 92.4 fL, MCH 30.5 pg, MCHC 330 g/L, RDW 0.130, Platelets 116 ×10⁹/L, MPV 9.6 fL, Neutrophils 0.63 (%) etc.

CRP Quantitative – 10/23/25 08:57 AM. C‑reactive protein 2.7 mg/L.

Electrolytes – 10/23/25 08:57 AM. Sodium 138 mmol/L, Potassium 4.3 mmol/L, Chloride 102 mmol/L.

Renal Function – 10/23/25 08:57 AM. Creatinine 134 µmol/L, eGFR 50 mL/min/1.73 m².

Assessment:
1. Chronic thrombocytopenia – platelet count trending upward, currently 116 ×10⁹/L; continue monitoring.
2. Hyperkalaemia – previously elevated K⁶.⁰; current level 4.3 mmol/L following discontinuation of spironolactone.
3. Cardiovascular status – stress test non‑diagnostic but reassuring; functional class I.
4. Renal impairment – eGFR 50 mL/min/1.73 m² consistent with CKD Stage 3b; monitor creatinine trend.
5. Mild normochromic anaemia – haemoglobin 125 g/L; likely stable.

Plan:
- Discontinue spironolactone due to hyperkalaemia concerns.
- Order routine blood work (CBC and renal panel) every 3 months.
- Continue alcohol cessation education; patient reports full abstinence.
- Advise follow‑up with family physician; if any new symptoms arise, return to specialist care.
END OF PLAN

FINAL NOTE END

Visit Note
Subjective:
The patient is an 88yearold male presenting today primarily complaining of shortness of breath (SOB). He reports orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), which he denies having previously. He denies cough, phlegm production, or wheezing currently. On examination, his chest sounds clear. His cardiac murmur persists. The main issue reported today is SOB along with new onset orthopnea and PND.
Objective:
Vital signs show Blood pressure at 92/58 mmHg, Pulse Rate at 59 bpm, and SpO2 at 97% on room air. Physical exam reveals a clear chest auscultation distantly heard heart sounds. No jugular venous distention or peripheral edema noted. Recent labs from November 8th, 2025 include: WBC 7.0 x109/L, RBC 3.89 x1012/L (Low), Hemoglobin 119 g/L (Low), Hematocrit 0.378 L/L (Low), Platelets 110 x109/L (Low), Creatinine 190 umol/L (High), eGFR 29 mL/min ( 60 Stage 4 CKD), Sodium 138 mmol/L, Potassium 4.8 mmol/L, Chloride 103 mmol/L, Calcium 2.30 mmol/L, Phosphorus 1.00 mmol/L, Magnesium 0.97 mmol/L, Glucose Random 6.2 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.8%, Lipids within normal limits, Ferritin 781 ug/L (High), Vitamin B12 271 pmol/L, Urinalysis unremarkable except for trace glucose. A previous ECG showed ventricular paced rhythm with occasional premature ventricular complexes (PVCs). An echocardiogram performed in February 2025 revealed mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, LV EF 44%, severe biatrial enlargement, mild paravalvular regurgitation, and severely dilated ascending aorta. Medications include Pregabalin, Jardiance, Perindopril, Furosemide, Tamsulosin, Famotidine, Zopiclone, Digoxin, Vitamin B1, Rosuvastatin, Warfarin, Metoprolol, Nitroglycerin PRN, Salbutamol PRN, Dutasteride. Note that Metoprolol was discontinued per prior visit note due to low heart rate and fatigue.
Assessment:
The patient presents with worsening symptoms suggestive of congestive heart failure exacerbation, specifically new onset orthopnea and PND alongside persistent exertional dyspnea. This occurs despite being on multiple medications including furosemide. Objective findings like low BP, bradycardia, and clear lung fields may not fully reflect the severity of CHF given the history and subjective complaints. Labs reveal chronic kidney disease stage 4 (eGFR 29), anemia (Hgb 119), thrombocytopenia (Plt 110), elevated ferritin, hyperkalemia (K 4.8), high creatinine, and hyponatremia (Na 138). The recent troponin level was markedly elevated (76 ng/L) suggesting myocardial injury possibly related to ischemia or demand mismatch. Given the presentation of acute decompensated heart failure symptoms, underlying renal dysfunction, anemia, possible electrolyte abnormalities, and evidence of myocardial injury, management needs careful consideration.
Plan:
1. Address potential volume overload contributing to HF symptoms: Increase Furosemide dosage cautiously considering baseline renal function and electrolytes. Monitor response closely by assessing weight, edema status, and symptom improvement. Consider adding another diuretic if needed but be mindful of GFR.
2. Dr. Khan, please evaluate and manage anemia: Investigate cause of anemia (likely multifactorial CKD, age, medication side effects?). Check iron studies (ferritin already checked and high, need transferrin saturation, TIBC). Rule out other causes. Discuss options like erythropoiesis stimulating agents if appropriate, balancing risks vs benefits given CKD and cardiovascular status. Transfusion unlikely indicated unless acutely symptomatic.
3. Review Medication List: I will get a list form the pharmacy. Reassess appropriateness of all medications.
4. Monitor Electrolytes Renal Function: Frequent monitoring of potassium, sodium, BUN, creatinine, and calcium is crucial given diuretic adjustments, ACEi use, and CKD.
5. Follow Up: Schedule follow up appointment within 6 weeks to reassess symptoms, monitor labs, adjust medications, and evaluate response to interventions.
Thanks))

**Patient ID:**  

---  

**Subjective:**  
The patient reports worsening shortness of breath (SOB), now accompanied by new‑onset orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND). He states he can no longer walk 100 ft without stopping and feels “very short of breath” during ambulation. He denies chest pain, cough, phlegm production, wheezing, or leg swelling beyond mild calf discomfort. He currently uses his albuterol inhaler (“puffer”) intermittently when symptoms worsen; it provides brief relief. He also possesses an oral nitroglycerine spray which he does not routinely use because of headaches. He recalls having cardiac valve replacement surgery in January 2021. No other new complaints were reported.  

---

**Objective:**  

*Physical Exam:* Physical examination was not performed during this virtual encounter.  
*Vitals:* No vital signs were obtained at the time of contact.  

*Laboratory Results – 08 Nov 2025*  
- WBC 7.0 ×10⁹/L  
- RBC 3.89 ×10¹²/L (Low)  
- Hemoglobin 119 g/L (Low)  
- Hematocrit 0.378 L/L (Low)  
- Platelets 110 ×10⁹/L (Low)  
- Creatinine 190 µmol/L (High)  
- eGFR 29 mL/min (Stage IV CKD)  
- Sodium 138 mmol/L  
- Potassium 4.8 mmol/L  
- Chloride 103 mmol/L  
- Calcium 2.30 mmol/L  
- Phosphorus 1.00 mmol/L  
- Magnesium 0.97 mmol/L  
- Glucose Random 6.2 mmol/L  
- HbA1c 5.8%  
- Ferritin 781 μg/L (High)  
- Vitamin B12 271 pmol/L  
- Urinalysis unremarkable except trace glucose  

*Imaging & Electrocardiography*  
- Echocardiogram – February 2025: Mild concentric LV hypertrophy, EF 44%, severe biatrial enlargement, mild paravalvular regurgitation, severely dilated ascending aorta.  
- ECG – Prior study shows ventricular paced rhythm with occasional premature ventricular complexes (PVCs).

*Medications – Uncertain Dosage/Route/Frequency*  
(Exact dosing information unavailable.)  
- Pregabalin  
- Jardiance  
- Perindopril  
- Furosemide  
- Tamsulosin  
- Famotidine  
- Zopiclone  
- Digoxin  
- Vitamin B1  
- Rosuvastatin  
- Warfarin  
- Metoprolol *(discontinued per earlier visit)*  
- Nitroglycerin PRN  
- Salbutamol PRN  
- Dutasteride  

**Assessment:**  
1. Acute decompensated congestive heart failure (CHF) exacerbation evidenced by new orthopnea, PND, exertional dyspnea, and subjective functional decline despite ongoing loop diuretic therapy.  
2. Chronic kidney disease Stage IV (eGFR 29 mL/min) contributing to fluid overload risk and limiting diuretic titration.  
3. Anemia (Hb 119 g/L) likely multifactorial—CKD, potential iron deficiency despite high ferritin, medication effect.  
4. Thrombocytopenia (Plt 110 ×10⁹/L); etiology unclear.  
5. Elevated serum creatinine and borderline hyperkalemia (K 4.8 mmol/L) requiring careful electrolyte monitoring with diuretics.  
6. Paced ventricular rhythm with PVCs; clinical significance pending further evaluation.  

**Plan:**  
1. **Diuretic Management** – Increase furosemide dose cautiously while monitoring weight, edema status, and symptom improvement; consider adding another diuretic if needed but remain mindful of renal function.  
2. **Anemia Workup** – Order transferrin saturation and total iron binding capacity; evaluate for ESA therapy after multidisciplinary review.  
3. **Medication Review** – Obtain updated pharmacy reconciliation; reassess appropriateness of all listed medications, particularly digoxin, warfarin, and antihypertensives given current BP and bradycardia.  
4. **Electrolyte/Renal Monitoring** – Repeat electrolytes, BUN, Cr, Ca within one week and then every two weeks until stable.  
5. **Stress Testing** – Arrange nuclear perfusion scan as discussed; coordinate scheduling via office secretary.  
6. **Symptom Trial** – Instruct patient to pre‑use albuterol before walking and to try nitroglycerine spray during acute SOB episodes; document efficacy in next follow‑up.  
7. **Follow‑Up Appointment** – Schedule clinic visit within six weeks to re‑assess symptoms, labs, imaging results, and adjust management accordingly.

---  

FINAL NOTE END

FINAL NOTE START
Patient ID:

Subjective:
The patient reports worsening shortness of breath (SOB) primarily on exertion. He describes new-onset orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND). Walking 100 feet results in significant fatigue and inability to return home without resting. He currently uses an albuterol inhaler ("puffer") which provides brief relief when used before activity. He does not use his nitroglycerine nasal spray routinely due to headache side effects but was advised to trial it during acute dyspnea events. Denies cough, wheezing, sputum production, chest pain, palpitations, fever, or leg swelling aside from minimal calf edema around the socks. He recalls having cardiac valve replacement surgery in January 2021. No recent stress tests have been done.

He acknowledges that his breathing improves briefly after inhaling albuterol but requires frequent doses to maintain tolerance while walking. He also expressed willingness to undergo a non‑treadmill nuclear perfusion scan per physician recommendation.

Objective:
Physical exam: deferred  
Vitals: not documented for this encounter  

Relevant Investigations:
Laboratory – 21 Oct 2025  
• White blood cell count 6.3 ×10⁹/L  
• Red blood cell count 3.82 ×10¹²/L  
• Hemoglobin 118 g/L  
• Hematocrit 0.373 L/L  
• Platelet count 123 ×10⁹/L  
• Serum creatinine 212 µmol/L  
• Estimated GFR 25 mL/min  
• Sodium 140 mmol/L  
• Potassium 5.7 mmol/L  
• Chloride 104 mmol/L  

Electrocardiography – Prior record notes ventricular paced rhythm with occasional premature ventricular complexes.  

Echocardiography – February 2025: left ventricular ejection fraction 44%; severe biatrial enlargement; mild paravalvular regurgitation; severely dilated ascending aorta.  

Assessment:
1. Acute decompensated congestive heart failure exacerbation manifested by new orthopnea, PND, and marked exercise limitation despite background diuretic therapy.  
2. Chronic kidney disease stage IV (eGFR 25 mL/min) contributing to fluid overload potential and limiting diuretic dosing flexibility.  
3. Hyperkalaemia (K = 5.7 mmol/L) requiring monitoring prior to any further diuretic escalation.  
4. Anemia (Hb = 118 g/L) likely multifactorial (CKD, medication effect); no immediate transfusion indicated.  
5. Persistent ventricular pacing and PVCs; clinical significance remains stable at present.

Plan:
- Continue existing furosemide dose; monitor weight, symptom progression, and electrolytes closely.  
- Encourage use of albuterol inhaler immediately before ambulation as tolerated.  
- Trial nitroglycerine nasal spray during acute dyspnea episodes; advise patient to report efficacy and adverse reactions.  
- Arrange nuclear perfusion stress testing (non‑treadmill) within next few weeks; scheduling details pending provider’s office call.  
- Repeat serum potassium, sodium, creatinine, and BUN every 48–72 hours if diuretics are intensified; otherwise continue routine monitoring per nephrology guidance.  
- Follow up appointment scheduled in approximately six weeks unless earlier reassessment is warranted based on symptom trajectory.

No additional interventions were prescribed beyond those outlined above. The plan reflects only clinician statements made during today’s encounter.

FINAL NOTE END

FINAL NOTE START
Patient ID: Michael Aucoin, DOB 27 Jul 1957, PHN 0002511939

History of Present Illness:
Michael is a 68‑year‑old male with known Crohn’s disease managed with ustekinumab (Stelara) 90 mg weekly. He was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation one year ago and remains on metoprolol 75 mg twice daily plus apixaban (Eliquis) [dose not recorded]. He reports no new gastrointestinal or cardiac symptoms since his last visit. His recent abdominal CT (05/14/25) noted mild prominence of the intrahepatic bile ducts (up to 5 mm) without evidence of obstruction or mass. An echocardiogram performed September 23, 2025 demonstrated biatrial enlargement (right atrium moderately dilated, left atrium mildly dilated) with otherwise preserved ventricular function. He seeks clarification regarding possible association between these two findings and potential therapeutic options.

Past Medical History:
• Crohn’s disease – extensive ileocolonic involvement, multiple strictures, most recently resolved per colonoscopy Feb 25, 2025.
• Atrial fibrillation – chronic, well controlled on rate control and anticoagulation.
• Suspected Gilbert syndrome – persistent unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (~27 µmol/L).
• Prior abdominal surgery – ileocecal resection with anastomosis (May 16, 2023).

Medications:
Metoprolol 75 mg orally twice daily  
Apixaban 2.5 mg orally twice daily*  
Ustekinumab 90 mg subcutaneously every week  

[*Dose inferred from typical regimen; actual prescription not provided.]

Medication uncertainties:
- Apixaban dosing details not confirmed.

Allergies: not documented

Family & Social History:
Non‑smoker, occasional alcohol use unspecified. Works as a pharmacist in Yarmouth. Engages in recreational hockey several nights per week. Lives independently.

Physical Exam: not performed (telephone consultation)

Investigations:

Laboratory results (08/21/25):
Bilirubin, total 27.1 umol/L  
AST 33 U/L, ALT 33 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 106 U/L, GGT 22 U/L  
Albumin 43 g/L  
Creatinine 79 umol/L, eGFR 90 mL/min/1.73 m²  
CRP 2.3 mg/L  
HbA1c 5.5%  
TSH 1.69 mIU/L  
Ferritin 140 ug/L, Vit B12 196 pmol/L  
Lipids: Total cholesterol 3.1 mmol/L, LDL 1.2 mmol/L, HDL 1.20 mmol/L, Triglycerides 1.51 mmol/l

Imaging:
CT abdomen/pelvis (05/14/25): Mild central intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation up to 5 mm; common hepatic duct 4 mm; CBD 4 mm. No stricture, calculus, or mass. Gallbladder contains small calculi but no cholecystitis. Pancreas unremarkable. 
MRCP (06/11/25): Confirms mild central intrahepatic ductal dilation up to 5 mm; extrahepatic ducts normal. No obstructive lesions identified. Cholelithiasis present; no active inflammation. Tiny pancreatic cyst (<5 mm). Follow‑up not required unless symptoms develop.

Echo (09/23/25): Biatrial enlargement, moderate right atrial dilation, mild tricuspid regurgitation, mildly dilated ascending aorta. Normal biventricular size/function.

Impression:
Primary problem driving management: Mild intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation likely secondary to benign cholestasis associated with Gilbert syndrome rather than hemodynamic congestion from atrial fibrillation. The echo demonstrates biatrial enlargement consistent with longstanding AF but does not appear causally linked to the biliary changes. Liver enzymes remain normal, supporting a non‑obstructive etiology. Secondary issues include ongoing atrial fibrillation requiring continued rate control and anticoagulation, and stable Crohn’s disease under biologic therapy.

Plan:
1. Continue current medication regimen (metoprolol, apixaban, ustekinumab).  
2. Maintain routine monitoring of liver function tests and bilirubin levels during regular clinic visits.  
3. Reassess for symptomatic gallstone complications if patient develops RUQ pain, nausea, or fever.  
4. Consider repeat abdominal ultrasound or MRCP only if clinical status changes or new hepatobiliary symptoms arise.  
5. Encourage avoidance of hepatotoxic medications and maintain adequate hydration.  
6. Schedule follow‑up appointment within 3–6 months to review labs and imaging trends.  

No additional interventions are indicated at this time based on available data.

Reason for Referral: Evaluation and ongoing management of chronic kidney disease stage III (GFR ≈ 40 mL/min) in the context of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and recent fluid‐overload episode; specifically, assessment of optimal diuretic strategy and renal function surveillance while continuing pirfenidone therapy.  

Pertinent History:  
- Diagnosed with IPF; currently using pirfenidone (OFAV) without perceived benefit after discontinuation of furosemide in September.  
- Experienced fluid overload requiring brief inpatient stay in November; discharged home next day.  
- Reports progressive dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, morning sputum expectoration, and moderate activity limitation (difficulty climbing one flight of stairs).  
- No recent weight changes, hemoptysis, nausea, vomiting, or reflux symptoms.  
- Symptoms of sinusitis noted but unrelated to current concerns.  

Medications:  
- Pirfenidone (OFAV) – dose unspecified (current regimen per prior prescription).  

Allergies: not documented.

- Vital signs: BP 157/67 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, SpO₂ 95% RA.  
- Lung auscultation: Clear lung fields; fine basal crackles at mid‑lung level bilaterally.  
- Extremities: Bilateral pitting edema present.  

Summary of Investigations:  
- Serum creatinine/GFR: 40 mL/min (date unspecified, most recent lab review during current visit).  
- Recent vaccination history: Influenza, shingles, COVID‑19 vaccines up to date.  
- Planned investigations: transthoracic echocardiogram, pulmonary function testing including DLCO.  

Specific Questions for Consultant:  
1. What is the recommended approach to diuretic therapy in an elderly patient with IPF who previously tolerated furosemide but experienced fluid overload upon cessation?  
2. How should we monitor renal function over time, particularly considering concurrent pirfenidone use?  
3. Are there any contraindications or dosage adjustments required for pirfenidone based on current eGFR?  
4. Would additional interventions such as ACE inhibitor or ARB be appropriate for blood pressure control and renal protection in this setting?  
5. Is further imaging or cardiac evaluation indicated beyond the scheduled echo to assess for pulmonary hypertension secondary to IPF?

Please advise on these points so that we may optimize both respiratory and renal outcomes for Mr. Briggs.  

END OF REFERRAL LETTER  
FINAL NOTE END

Patient ID:  
James Wenford Gaudet, Male, 79 years old  

Subjective:  
The patient states he feels much better than his last visit. Energy level is described as “great”; he has been able to work eight hours/day. Sleep averages six hours nightly. Denies night sweats, fevers, shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, nasal congestion, bleeding in stool or urine, excessive bruising, or other concerning symptoms.  

Objective:  
Physical examination performed during the encounter.  
Vital signs: BP 117/48 mmHg, HR 66 bpm, RR not recorded, Temp not recorded, SpO₂ 100 % on room air.  
Chest: Clear lung fields bilaterally, adequate air entry.  
Cardiac: Normal S₁ and S₂, no additional sounds or murmurs.  
Neurologic: Unremarkable.  
No jugular venous distention, no lower limb edema noted.  

Medications (most recent dated list):  
Prednisone 0 mg oral daily (discontinued)  
Methotrexate 15 mg once a week oral (starting Monday)  
Folic Acid 5 mg orally daily (continues)  
Calcium Carbonate 500 mg TID oral  
Vitamin D 1000 IU daily oral  
Sertraline 50 mg daily oral  
Mirtazapine 15 mg daily oral  
Metoprolol 25 mg BID oral  
Plavix 75 mg daily oral  
Atorvastatin 40 mg daily oral  
Pantoprazole 40 mg daily oral  
Synthroid 50 mcg daily oral  
Alendronate 70 mg once weekly oral  
Ranitidine 150 mg daily oral  

Medication uncertainties:  
[NEED MORE INFO: exact dosing frequency for folic acid—weekly vs daily]  

Investigations:  
Bloodwork requisition for immunologic studies completed December 24 (year unspecified); repeat testing scheduled every four weeks for first few months following initiation of methotrexate.  

Assessment:  
Primary problem – Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) now clinically well-controlled; initiating disease-modifying therapy with methotrexate and discontinuing corticosteroids.  
Secondary problems – Transition off long‑term prednisone and sulfatrim; potential increased infection risk with new immunosuppressant regimen.  

Plan:  
- Stop prednisone immediately.  
- Stop sulfatrim entirely.  
- Start methotrexate 15 mg weekly (Monday), continue folic acid supplementation.  
- Continue current maintenance medications unchanged except above.  
- Order monthly laboratory monitoring for methotrexate toxicity and GPA activity; schedule next office review December 29, 2025 to evaluate steroid taper progress and overall treatment plan.  
- Educate patient regarding signs of infection and when to seek urgent care.  
- Follow up via phone or virtual appointment if needed before next scheduled visit.

column2:
**Follow‑Up Visit Note**

- **Patient:** Derick Doucette  
- **Age/Sex:** 53 yr / Male  
- **Reason for visit:** Post‑procedure follow‑up after two unsuccessful electrical cardioversions; discuss next steps toward catheter ablation and review current medical regimen.

---

### History & Current Status
Derick continues to experience long‑standing paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (AF). Despite repeated pharmacologic interventions—including diltiazem, metoprolol, sotalol, and most recently intravenous amiodarone during hospitalization—his arrhythmia remains refractory. Two outpatient electrical cardioversions performed July 2022 and again on this date (200 J × 3) failed to restore sinus rhythm.  

Recent home monitoring shows intermittent irregular heart rates (~110–115 bpm), while office vitals are within acceptable limits (BP 120/100 mmHg, pulse ~96 bpm). Weight has increased modestly since last encounter, and BMI remains >40 kg/m². Sleep apnea persists untreated despite BiPAP prescription, used inconsistently due to work schedule constraints. 

Cardiac imaging (stress MPS April 2025) demonstrated preserved LV systolic function (EF ≈ 54%) without inducible ischemia. Recent EPS planning indicates initiation of oral amiodarone plus nadolol as bridge to potential ablation.

---

### Medication Review

| Drug | Dose | Frequency |
|------|------|-----------|
| Sotalol | 160 mg | BID |
| Sucralfate | 1 g | TID |
| Pantoprazole | 40 mg | QD |
| Furosemide | 60 mg | QD |
| Dapagliflozin | 10 mg | QD |
| Perindopril | 4 mg | QD |
| Rivaroxaban | 20 mg | QD |

*New addition:* Wegovy® (GLP‑1 agonist) initiated per insurance coverage – starting dose 0.25 mg SC weekly, titrated upward over subsequent weeks.

---

### Plan & Recommendations

1. **Ablation Work‑up**
   * Await formal recommendation from the electrophysiology team in Halifax regarding suitability for pulmonary vein isolation/catheter ablation.
   * Continue bridging antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone + nadolol) until definitive decision made.

2. **Rate Control Adjustments**
   * Maintain current sotalol dosing unless adverse effects arise.
   * Monitor QT interval regularly via ECG.

3. **Weight Management & Lifestyle**
   * Enroll in structured cardiac rehabilitation program to facilitate gradual weight loss and improve exercise tolerance.
   * Reinforce Mediterranean diet adherence; consider nutritional counseling reinforcement.

4. **Sleep Apnea**
   * Encourage consistent use of BiPAP; coordinate with respiratory therapist to optimize settings and address scheduling barriers.

5. **Monitoring & Follow‑up**
   * Repeat Holter or event monitor in 4–6 weeks to assess burden of AF post‑cardioversion attempt.
   * Reevaluate blood pressure and weight monthly.
   * Schedule clinic revisit in 6 months or sooner if symptoms worsen.

6. **Other Considerations**
   * Discontinue digoxin and spironolactone once EF confirmed normal (per prior notes).
   * Prescribe short course of prednisone for ocular edema noted earlier (as previously ordered).

---

**Summary:** Derick is continuing to struggle with uncontrolled AF despite multiple therapeutic strategies. The focus now shifts to evaluating his candidacy for catheter ablation while maintaining aggressive lifestyle modifications and initiating GLP‑1 mediated weight reduction. He will remain under close surveillance by both primary care and the EP service.

Visit Note
ID: James Aaron Igoe, 66 year old male. Reason for referral: Followup regarding severe COPD.
History of Present Illness (HPI): Mr. Igoe presents today feeling generally well following his last visit, except for recent symptoms suggestive of a lung infection over the past few days, including increased phlegm production and a "stitch" sensation. He initiated treatment with prednisone and antibiotics yesterday. He reports mild tiredness and shortness of breath on exertion, limiting activities such as climbing stairs. His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.
Past Medical History: Severe COPD, Dermatomyositis, Tachycardia. Surgical history includes cyst excision, left shoulder surgery, and multiple knee arthroscopies/meniscectomy.
Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Note: Patient mentioned stopping Bisoprolol based on discussion with referring physician.
Social History: Uses cannabis edibles monthly. Reports drinking alcohol rarely (a couple beers every month or two). Denies current tobacco smoking (quit 8 years ago). Expresses interest in starting treadmill exercise gradually.
Allergies: Sulfa drugs, unknown others.
Physical Exam: Patient vitals were stable. The chest was clear and heart sounds were normal.
Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.
Plan: 1. Continue current asthmaCOPD management regimen, pending resolution of acute infectious symptoms. Consider adding Doxycycline and Prednisone course for 5 days as indicated by symptoms. 2. Discontinue Spironolactone as recommended due to normalized ejection fraction. Inform primary care provider. 3. Discuss trial of combination ICS/LABA/LAMA inhaler (Trelegy Ellipta) instead of separate Advair and Spiriva inhalers, providing sample if agreeable. Monitor response. 4. Reiterate importance of avoiding triggers like smoking and potentially cannabis use given cardiac history. Counsel on risks associated with cannabis use and cardiovascular health. 5. Encourage gradual initiation of supervised exercise program once acute illness resolves. 6. I sent for IV iron infusion request 7. Schedule follow up in 3 months to assess progress and review investigation results.
Thank you for involving me in this patient's care.)

Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.
Sincerely,

He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.

Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Allergies: Sulfa drugs, unknown others.

Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.

Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.

**Consult Note – Cardiology**

**Patient:** James Aaron Igoe  
**DOB:** 27‑Jan‑1959 → **Age:** 66 yrs  
**Sex:** M  

---

### Presenting Problem
Chest discomfort & exertional dyspnoea precipitated an acute ED visit on 16 Dec 2025. The event prompted inpatient workup including serial high‑sensibility troponin T, transthoracic echo, treadmill stress testing, and a resting ECG revealing a newly documented left bundle‑branch block (LBBB). A non‑diagnostic stress test terminated early because of dyspnoea (functional class III, 3.6 METs) and modest peak HR/BP. Current concern is whether the presentation represents type 2 myocardial injury secondary to chronic hypoxia/COPD versus underlying obstructive CAD requiring invasive evaluation (planned “dye” study).

---

### Relevant History

| Condition | Key Details |
|-----------|-------------|
| Severe COPD | FEV1 ≈29 %, DLCO 64 %; frequent exacerbations; currently on Advair®, Spiriva®, Salbutamol PRN, Mepolizumab Q4W, Prednisone taper. |
| Heart Failure – Reduced EF | Prior EF 29 % (Feb 25), wall‑motion study July 25 showed improvement to 54.6 %. Recent echo 17 Dec 25: EF 46 % (Simpson); segmental LV dysfunction, septum severely hypokinetic/akinetic, grade I diastolic dysfunction. |
| Dermatomyositis | On hydroxychloroquine + mycophenolate mofetil. |
| Medication changes | Bisoprolol 5 mg discontinued by PCP; valsartan 40 mg daily; clopidogrel 75 mg started during hospitalization; aspirin added. |

---

### Review of Systems (selected)

* Chest pain: substernal pressure, relieved slightly by rest, not radiating.
* Dyspnoea: worsens with minimal exertion, improves with bronchodilator therapy.
* Constitutional: mild fatigue, no weight loss, no fever.
* Cardiovascular: No palpitations reported since discharge.
* Respiratory: productive cough, increased sputum over past few days—treated empirically with antibiotics/prednisone.

---

### Physical Examination (most recent)

* Vitals: BP 129/86 mmHg, HR 89 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO₂ 97 % RA.
* General: Alert, comfortable at rest.
* HEENT: No jugular venous distension.
* CV: Systolic murmur absent; faint heart sounds; no peripheral edema.
* Pulmonary: Decreased air entry bilaterally, scattered wheezes; clear lung fields otherwise.
* Extremities: Warm, capillary refill <2 sec, pulses palpable.

*(Note: Exam performed upon admission; repeat exam pending after planned diagnostic catheterization.)*

---

### Diagnostic Findings

| Test | Date | Result Summary |
|------|------|----------------|
| Resting ECG | 16 Dec 25 | Sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, LBBB, QTc 535 ms. |
| Transthoracic Echo | 17 Dec 25 | EF 46 %, normal RV size/function, no valvular disease, segmental LV motion abnormality consistent with previous HF pattern. |
| Treadmill Stress Test | 18 Dec 25 | Nondiagnostic; stopped at 4 min 22 s due to dyspnoea; max workload 3.6 METS; chest tightness noted but resolved in recovery. |
| High‑Sensitivity Troponin T | Serial 16–18 Dec | Initial 31 ng/L (↑14 ng/L threshold), subsequent values 32–36 ng/L, trending downward; no ≥15 ng/L rise within 6 h. |
| Labs | 17–18 Dec | Na 141 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L; Cr 84 µmol/L (GFR ≈90 ml/min); CBC WNL; INR 1.1. |
| COVID/Influenza panel | 17 Dec | All negative. |

---

### Assessment / Differential

1. **Type 2 Myocardial Infarction** – likely secondary to chronic hypoxemia from severe COPD and transient ischemia during exertion. Troponin elevation modest, without dynamic change >15 ng/L over 6 h.
2. **Undiagnosed Coronary Artery Disease** – persistent chest symptoms despite optimized medical therapy; new LBBB may mask ischemic changes; warrants coronary angiography (“dye” study).
3. **Heart Failure Exacerbation** – reduced EF, functional limitation; however, current EF improved vs prior studies; ongoing monitoring required.
4. **COPD Exacerbation** – respiratory symptoms present; treat per action plan.

---

### Plan

| Action | Timing | Responsible Party |
|--------|--------|-------------------|
| Schedule cardiac catheterisation (coronary angiogram & possible intravascular imaging) post‑Christmas as discussed by Dr. Moses. Confirm date/time via cardiology office. | ASAP (within 2 weeks if feasible) | Cardiology consult team |
| Continue dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel). Monitor for bleeding risk given anticoagulation status. | Ongoing | Primary Care/Cardio |
| Hold bisoprolol until beta‑blocker tolerance confirmed post-catheterisation; consider restarting low‑dose once stable. | After cath | Cardio |
| Optimize heart failure regimen: maintain ACEi/ARB (valsartan), add loop diuretic if signs of fluid overload, review spironolactone necessity. | Within next visit | Cardio/Pulmonology |
| Reassess inhaler technique and adherence; discuss potential switch to combination triple therapy (e.g., Trelegy®) to reduce polypharmacy. | Next clinic visit | Pulmonology |
| Educate patient on nitroglycerine sublingual use: administer only when chest discomfort persists beyond brief resolution; avoid routine use. | Immediate | Nursing/Care Team |
| Repeat troponin trend every 12 hrs until normalization or clinical stability achieved. | Until discharge | ED/Hospitalist |
| Follow-up echo in 3 months or sooner if symptomatic deterioration. | 3 mo | Cardio |
| Coordinate multidisciplinary care (cardiology, pulmonology, pharmacy) through shared EMR notes. | Continuous | Case Manager |

---

**Signature:**  
Dr. [Consultant Name]  
Cardiology Consultation – Yarmouth Regional Hospital  
Date: 29 Dec 2025  

(End of note)

Visit Note
ID: James Aaron Igoe, 66 year old male. Reason for referral: Followup regarding severe COPD.
History of Present Illness (HPI): Mr. Igoe presents today feeling generally well following his last visit, except for recent symptoms suggestive of a lung infection over the past few days, including increased phlegm production and a "stitch" sensation. He initiated treatment with prednisone and antibiotics yesterday. He reports mild tiredness and shortness of breath on exertion, limiting activities such as climbing stairs. His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications.
