Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.

Visit Note
ID: James Aaron Igoe, 66 year old male. Reason for referral: Followup regarding severe COPD.
History of Present Illness (HPI): Mr. Igoe presents today feeling generally well following his last visit, except for recent symptoms suggestive of a lung infection over the past few days, including increased phlegm production and a "stitch" sensation. He initiated treatment with prednisone and antibiotics yesterday. He reports mild tiredness and shortness of breath on exertion, limiting activities such as climbing stairs. His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.
Past Medical History: Severe COPD, Dermatomyositis, Tachycardia. Surgical history includes cyst excision, left shoulder surgery, and multiple knee arthroscopies/meniscectomy.
Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Note: Patient mentioned stopping Bisoprolol based on discussion with referring physician.
Social History: Uses cannabis edibles monthly. Reports drinking alcohol rarely (a couple beers every month or two). Denies current tobacco smoking (quit 8 years ago). Expresses interest in starting treadmill exercise gradually.
Allergies: Sulfa drugs, unknown others.
Physical Exam: Patient vitals were stable. The chest was clear and heart sounds were normal.
Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.
Plan: 1. Continue current asthmaCOPD management regimen, pending resolution of acute infectious symptoms. Consider adding Doxycycline and Prednisone course for 5 days as indicated by symptoms. 2. Discontinue Spironolactone as recommended due to normalized ejection fraction. Inform primary care provider. 3. Discuss trial of combination ICS/LABA/LAMA inhaler (Trelegy Ellipta) instead of separate Advair and Spiriva inhalers, providing sample if agreeable. Monitor response. 4. Reiterate importance of avoiding triggers like smoking and potentially cannabis use given cardiac history. Counsel on risks associated with cannabis use and cardiovascular health. 5. Encourage gradual initiation of supervised exercise program once acute illness resolves. 6. I sent for IV iron infusion request 7. Schedule follow up in 3 months to assess progress and review investigation results.
Thank you for involving me in this patient's care.)

Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.
Sincerely,

**Consult Note – Cardiology**

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| **Name:** | James A. Igoe |
| **DOB:** | 27 Jan 1959 |
| **Age:** | 66 years (as of 18 Dec 2025) |
| **Sex:** | Male |
| **Referral Source:** Internal Medicine/Hospitalist (Dr Moses) |
| **Date of Consultation:** 18 Dec 2025 |

---

### Reason for Referral  
Evaluation of an acute episode of chest pain and exertional dyspnoea occurring two weeks ago while travelling from Marshalltown to Yarmouth. The patient has a complex cardiovascular history (previously HFrEF, now EF ≈ 46 %) and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Recent investigations include a non‑diagnostic treadmill stress test, transthoracic echocardiogram, serial high‑sensitivity troponin T measurements, and a resting electrocardiogram showing persistent left bundle branch block (LBBB).

The primary clinical question is whether the presentation represents a type II myocardial injury related to COPD exacerbation or a genuine acute coronary syndrome requiring definitive revascularisation (“dye” study planned post‑Christmas).

---

### Relevant History  

* **Cardiovascular:**  
  * Prior HFpEF → EF 29 % Feb 2025, improved to 54–55 % July 2025, currently 46 %.  
  * Non‑diagnostic Bruce protocol stress test (max HR 107 bpm, max BP 119/78 mmHg, 3.6 METs); stopped early due to dyspnoea and chest tightness.  
  * Resting ECG: sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, LBBB.  
  * Serial hs‑troponin T: 31→36→33 ng/L over 24 h (peak < 50 ng/L, Δ < 15 ng/L). No clear evidence of evolving MI.  
  * Echocardiogram (17 Dec): LV EF 46 %; grade I diastolic dysfunction; segmental hypokinesis of anterior/septal walls; LBBB noted.  

* **Pulmonary:** Severe COPD (FEV1 29 %, FEV1/FVC 36 %). Current inhalers: Advair, Spiriva, Salbutamol PRN, Prednisone courses as needed.  

* **Medications (current at time of consult)**  
  * Bisoprolol 5 mg QD  
  * Valacyclovir 1000 mg QD  
  * Dapagliflozin 10 mg QD  
  * Spironolactone discontinued (per previous note)  
  * Mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg BID  
  * Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD  
  * Prednisone (intermittent)  
  * Aspirin 81 mg daily (started during admission)  
  * Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC q12 hrs (initiated by inpatient team)  

* **Social / Lifestyle**  
  * Former smoker (quit > 25 yrs ago).  
  * Occasional cannabis edibles.  
  * Rare alcohol intake.  

---

### Assessment

1. **Acute Chest Pain/Dyspnoea** – likely multifactorial:
   * Type II myocardial injury secondary to severe COPD exacerbation & hypoxia.
   * Possible underlying epicardial CAD given age, risk factors, and recent LBBB.
2. **Non‑Diagnostic Stress Test** – limited interpretability because of baseline LBBB and low functional capacity (3.6 METs).
3. **Echocardiographic Findings** – mild systolic impairment with regional wall‐motion abnormalities; no valvular lesions.
4. **Troponin Trend** – modest elevation without dynamic change; does not meet universal definition of NSTEMI but warrants continued monitoring.
5. **Medication Review** – β‑blocker tolerated; aspirin + fondaparinux appropriate for possible ischemia; consider holding nitrates unless angina persists.

---

### Plan

| Action | Timing | Responsible Party |
|--------|--------|-------------------|
| **Continue current medical regimen** (β‑blocker, ACEi/ARB, anticoagulation, antiplatelet) | Throughout hospitalization | Inpatient team |
| **Serial hs‑troponin T every 6 hr until stable** | Until discharge | Lab / Nursing |
| **Repeat bedside echo if clinically indicated** | As needed | Cardiologist |
| **Schedule invasive coronary angiography ("dye" study)** | Post‑Christmas per Dr Moses’ plan; confirm date/time via cardiology office | Cardiology Coordinator |
| **Optimize COPD therapy** – review inhaler technique, consider adding triple therapy (ICS/LABA/LAMA) once cardiac status clarified | Within next clinic visit | Pulmonology referral |
| **Counsel on cannabis use** – discuss potential arrhythmogenic effects in context of LBBB and heart failure | During follow‑up | Primary Care |
| **Discontinue unnecessary medications** – e.g., spironolactone already stopped; evaluate need for mycophenolate/moeif? | Ongoing | Rheumatology/Pulmonology |
| **Follow‑up appointment** – within 2 weeks after discharge or sooner if symptoms recur | Schedule outpatient visit | Clinic Scheduler |

---

### Summary
James A. Igoe presents with an episode of exertional chest pain and dyspnea that has been investigated with a non‑diagnostic stress test, echocardiography, and serial troponins. The data suggest a probable type II myocardial injury associated with his severe COPD rather than a classic NSTEMI, although underlying coronary artery disease cannot be excluded. He will remain on dual antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation while awaiting results from the scheduled coronary angiography. His pulmonary management will continue to be optimized concurrently. Follow‑up is arranged promptly to reassess clinical status and imaging findings.

He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.

Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.

He initiated treatment with prednisone and antibiotics yesterday. He reports mild tiredness and shortness of breath on exertion, limiting activities such as climbing stairs. His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.
Past Medical History: Severe COPD, Dermatomyositis, Tachycardia. Surgical history includes cyst excision, left shoulder surgery, and multiple knee arthroscopies/meniscectomy.
Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Note: Patient mentioned stopping Bisoprolol based on discussion with referring physician.
Social History: Uses cannabis edibles monthly. Reports drinking alcohol rarely (a couple beers every month or two). Denies current tobacco smoking (quit 8 years ago). Expresses interest in starting treadmill exercise gradually.
Allergies: Sulfa drugs, unknown others.
Physical Exam: Patient vitals were stable. The chest was clear and heart sounds were normal.
Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.
Plan: 1. Continue current asthmaCOPD management regimen, pending resolution of acute infectious symptoms. Consider adding Doxycycline and Prednisone course for 5 days as indicated by symptoms. 2. Discuss trial of combination ICS/LABA/LAMA inhaler (Trelegy Ellipta) instead of separate Advair and Spiriva inhalers, providing sample if agreeable. Monitor response. 4. Reiterate importance of avoiding triggers like smoking and potentially cannabis use given cardiac history. Counsel on risks associated with cannabis use and cardiovascular health. 5. Encourage gradual initiation of supervised exercise program once acute illness resolves. 6. I sent for IV iron infusion request 7. Schedule follow up in 3 months to assess progress and review investigation results.
Thank you for involving me in this patient's care.)

**Consultation Note – Yarmouth Regional Hospital**

**Patient:** James Aaron Igoe  
**DOB:** 27 Jan 1959 | **Age:** 66 yrs | **Sex:** Male  

---

### Chief Complaint / Reason for Referral
Chest discomfort & dyspnea of recent onset during exertion, evaluated by inpatient internal medicine service following an acute episode of angina‑like symptoms. The admitting team has ordered further cardiac assessment (“dye” angiography) scheduled shortly after Christmas.

---

## History of Present Illness
* 66‑year‑old man with severe COPD (FEV1 29%, FEV1/FVC 36%) and a history of heart failure with reduced EF (EF ≈30% Feb 2025, improved to ~55–60% July 2025).
* Presented two weeks ago with chest tightness while walking down the hallway; symptom resolved spontaneously.
* Admitted to hospital where he received fondaparinux, dual antiplatelet therapy, aspirin, and a nitroglycerine spray (not yet used since discharge).
* Troponins were mildly elevated initially (31 ng/L) but serial values trended downward; repeat troponins not yet performed.
* Stress test (modified Bruce protocol) terminated early at 3.6 METs due to dyspnea; non‑diagnostic because of pre‑existing left bundle branch block (LBBB). Functional class III.
* Echo (12 Dec 25) shows LV EF 46.2 % (Simpson), mild diastolic dysfunction grade I, segmental wall motion abnormalities—severe hypokinesis of septum/confounded by conduction delay; normal RV size/function, no valvular disease.
* ECG (16 Dec 25): sinus rhythm, LBBB present, QTc 535 ms.
* Labs: Na 141 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Cl 103 mmol/L; creatinine 84 µmol/L (eGFR >90); glucose random 7.0 mmol/L; INR 1.1; CBC within limits except borderline neutropenia (neutrophils 0.76 ×10⁹/L).

---

## Past Medical History
| Condition | Notes |
|-----------|-------|
| Severe COPD | Chronic bronchitis/emphysema, frequent exacerbations |
| Heart Failure w/RF | Prior EF <35%; current EF ~45–50% |
| Dermatomyositis | On immunosuppression (mycophenolate, hydroxychloroquine) |
| Atrial fibrillation? | Not documented; currently on bisoprolol (now discontinued per PCP) |
| Diabetes mellitus type II | Random glucose 7.0 mmol/L; HbA1c unknown |
| Hypertension | Managed with valsartan 40 mg daily |
| Hyperlipidemia | Previously rosuvastatin (discontinued) |

Medications (current as of 18 Dec 25):
- Valsartan 40 mg QD
- Bisoprolol 5 mg QD (recently stopped)
- Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg qid PRN
- Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID
- Advair (fluticasone/salmeterol) 250 µg BID
- Mepolizumab 100 mg every 4 wk
- Prednisone taper (as needed for COPD flare)
- Mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg BID
- Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD
- Dapagliflozin 10 mg QD
- Aspirin 81 mg daily (post‑admission)

All other chronic medications are unchanged.

---

## Review of Systems
Positive: Dyspnea on exertion, intermittent chest tightness, occasional cough with sputum production. Negative: No orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, weight loss, fever, palpitations, syncope, rash, GI bleeding.

---

## Physical Examination (Hospital Admission)
Vitals stable: BP 129/86 mmHg, HR 89 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO₂ 97% RA. Cardiac exam: faint S₁/S₂, no murmurs. Pulmonary: decreased breath sounds bilaterally, scattered wheezes. Abdomen soft, nontender. Extremities warm, pulses palpable, minimal edema.

---

## Assessment

1. **Stable ischemic heart disease / possible ACS** – Chest pain with dyspnea in setting of known coronary risk factors and abnormal baseline ECG (LBBB). Non‑diagnostic stress test; echo suggests regional wall motion abnormalities. Pending invasive coronary angiogram will clarify anatomy and guide revascularization if indicated.

2. **Heart failure with preserved/mildly reduced ejection fraction** – Current EF 46%. Symptomatic functional Class III; likely volume overload from COPD exacerbation or arrhythmogenic substrate. Continue ACEi/ARB, beta‑blocker (once tolerated), diuretics as needed.

3. **COPD** – Persistent airflow limitation; recent exacerbation treated successfully. Maintain inhaled bronchodilator regimen; consider stepping up to triple therapy (ICS + LABA + LAMA) if symptoms persist despite current combination.

4. **Dermatomyositis & Immunosuppression** – Stable on mycophenolate/hydroxychloroquine; monitor for infection risk given concurrent steroids.

5. **Hypertension** – Controlled on valsartan; continue monitoring.

6. **Mild renal impairment** – Creatinine 84 µmol/L; adjust nephrotoxic drugs accordingly.

---

## Plan

### Immediate Management
- **Cardiology follow‑up:** Arrange cardiac catheterization (“dye” study) post‑Christmas as requested by Dr. Moses. Coordinate scheduling through cardiology office; confirm date/time via phone/email.
- **Troponin trend:** Repeat high‑sensitivity Troponin T next morning; compare serial values over 6 h intervals to rule out evolving MI.
- **Medication review:**  
  - Resume bisoprolol once hemodynamics permit (target HR <70 bpm).  
  - Consider low‑dose loop diuretic (furosemide 20 mg PO bid) if signs of fluid retention appear.  
  - Continue aspirin 81 mg daily; hold clopidogrel until after cath unless stent placed.  
  - Reinforce nitroglycerine spray use: instruct patient to administer sublingually at onset of angina, wait 5 min, repeat twice more if unresolved; advise not to exceed three doses per episode.
- **Pulmonology consult:** Evaluate need for pulmonary rehab program; discuss potential adjustment to inhalers (possible switch to Trelegy Ellipta).
- **Diabetes management:** Order fasting glucose/HbA1c tomorrow; adjust metformin dose if necessary.

### Long‑Term Follow‑Up
- **Post‑angiography care:** Discuss results, potential PCI/CABG, medical optimization.  
- **CHF management:** Target BNP ≤150 pg/ml; titrate ARB/ACEI and add mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist only if EF remains below 55% and potassium stays <5.0 mmol/L.  
- **COPD control:** Reassess spirometry in 3–6 months; evaluate for home oxygen if resting O₂ <88%.  
- **Immunosuppressive surveillance:** CBC, CMP weekly during steroid taper; screen for opportunistic infections before any escalation.  

### Patient Education
- Reviewed indications for nitroglycerin spray and proper technique.  
- Advised against abrupt cessation of beta‑blocker due to underlying HF.  
- Encouraged adherence to inhaler schedule; discussed importance of avoiding triggers such as smoking, cold air, and allergens.  
- Informed about signs warranting urgent evaluation: worsening chest pain, new SOB, dizziness, palpitations.

### Documentation & Coordination
- Notify PCP Joel Phimarsenault of pending cath and medication adjustments.  
- Send copy of this note to inpatient team, cardiology, pulmonology, pharmacy, and the patient's primary clinic.  
- Schedule outpatient visit two weeks after cath to review findings and modify treatment plan.

His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.
Past Medical History: Severe COPD, Dermatomyositis, Tachycardia. Surgical history includes cyst excision, left shoulder surgery, and multiple knee arthroscopies/meniscectomy.
Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.

have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.

quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.

most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as

palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.

His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.
Past Medical History: Severe COPD, Dermatomyositis, Tachycardia. Surgical history includes cyst excision, left shoulder surgery, and multiple knee arthroscopies/meniscectomy.
Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Allergies: Sulfa drugs, unknown others.
Physical Exam: Patient vitals were stable. The chest was clear and heart sounds were normal.
Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.
Plan: 1. Continue current asthmaCOPD management regimen, pending resolution of acute infectious symptoms. Consider adding Doxycycline and Prednisone course for 5 days as indicated by symptoms. 2. Discontinue Spironolactone as recommended due to normalized ejection fraction. Inform primary care provider. 3. Discuss trial of combination ICS/LABA/LAMA inhaler (Trelegy Ellipta) instead of separate Advair and Spiriva inhalers, providing sample if agreeable. Monitor response. 4. Reiterate importance of avoiding triggers like smoking and potentially cannabis use given cardiac history. Counsel on risks associated with cannabis use and cardiovascular health. 5. Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.
Sincerely,

**Consult Note – Cardiology** **Patient:** James Aaron Igoe **DOB:** 27 Jan 1959 → **Age:** 66 y **Sex:** Male --- ### Reason for Referral The patient presents with recurrent exertional chest discomfort and short‑of‑breath that prompted an inpatient admission for suspected acute coronary syndrome (ACS) and subsequent non‑diagnostic treadmill stress testing. An invasive coronary angiogram (“dye” study) has been scheduled by the admitting team following the most recent cardiac work‑up. The primary goal of this consultation is to: 1. Review current cardiovascular status and laboratory trends. 2. Optimize medical therapy for ischemia/HF while awaiting definitive imaging. 3. Address potential drug interactions and contraindications related to his complex comorbidities (COPD, HFrEF, dermatomyositis). 4. Provide guidance on peri‑procedural management surrounding the forthcoming coronary angiogram. --- ## History & Current Status | Item | Findings | |------|----------| | **Chief Complaint** | Exertional chest tightness/pain + dyspnea; resolved partially with nitroglycerine spray; intermittent episodes since last admission. | | **Recent Hospital Course** | Admitted 16–20 Dec 2025 for chest pain; received IV nitro, fondaparinux, dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel); discharged home with instructions pending angiographic assessment. | | **Cardiac Tests** | • **Treadmill Stress Test** – MOD.BRUCE protocol; terminated at 4 min 22 s due to dyspnea; max HR 107 bpm (~69 % predicted), max BP 119/78 mmHg; functional class III, 3.6 METS.
• **Echo (12 Feb 2025)** – EF 46 %, segmental wall motion abnormality (severely hypokinetic septum, anterior wall), Grade I diastolic dysfunction, LBBB noted.
• **ECG (16 Dec 2025)** – Normal sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, persistent LBBB. | | **Troponin Trend** | Serial hs‑troponin T values: 31 ng/L (initial), 32–36 ng/L over next days; trend suggests type‑II myocardial injury likely secondary to demand/supply mismatch rather than plaque rupture. | | **Renal Function** | Creatinine 84 µmol/L (eGFR ~90 mL/min/1.73m²) – adequate for contrast exposure. | | **Pulmonary Status** | Severe COPD (FEV₁ 29 %; FEV₁/FVC 36 %) with chronic bronchospasm; on inhaled LABA/LAMA combination (Advair + Spiriva), rescue albuterol, oral steroids as needed. | | **Other Medications** | Bisoprolol 5 mg QD (currently held per prior discussion), valsartan 40 mg QD, mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg BID, hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, prednisolone tapering, clopidogrel 75 mg QD (started 21 Dec 2025), low‑dose aspirin (not in pharmacy record but presumed continued), dapagliflozin 10 mg QD, spironolactone discontinued after EF improvement. | --- ## Assessment 1. **Stable, probable type‑II MI / supply/demand imbalance** – troponins modestly elevated, no ongoing ischemia on telemetry or repeat ECG, and clinical picture consistent with COPD flare plus deconditioning. 2. **Reduced but improving LV systolic function** – EF 46 % vs previous 54.6 %. Segmental wall motion abnormalities persist, likely related to conduction delay (LBBB). No valvular disease. 3. **Left bundle branch block** – complicating interpretation of ECG changes; may mask subtle ST/T wave alterations. 4. **Chronic severe COPD** – contributes to hypoxia, hypercapnia, and increased pulmonary vascular resistance; requires careful peri‑operative oxygen titration. 5. **Polypharmacy & immunosuppression** – risk of infection, bleeding, drug interactions (especially anticoagulants). --- ## Plan | Action | Details | Timing | |--------|---------|-------| | **Antithrombotic Therapy** | Maintain dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel) through angiography. Hold fondaparinux once DAPT established. Discuss bridging strategy if urgent PCI anticipated. | Pre‑procedure | | **Beta‑Blocker Reintroduction** | Resume bisoprolol 2.5 mg PO daily (titrate up to 5 mg) unless contraindicated by bradycardia/hypotension. Monitor heart rate/BP post‑angiogram. | Within 48 h | | **ACEi/ARB Optimization** | Continue valsartan 40 mg QD; consider adding a low‑dose loop diuretic if signs of fluid overload appear during hospitalization. | Ongoing | | **Statin Resumption** | Restart rosuvastatin 10 mg nightly given ASCVD risk profile and upcoming revascularization. | Prior to discharge | | **COPD Management** | Verify adherence to LABA/LAMA combo; reinforce use of rescue albuterol. Consider short course prednisone only when exacerbation confirmed. Ensure influenza & pneumococcal vaccination status updated. | Continuous | | **Peri‑Procedural Oxygen** | Target SpO₂ ≥94 % using nasal cannula; avoid high FiO₂ that could worsen CO₂ retention. | During cath | | **Monitoring Troponin** | Repeat hs‑troponin T 0, 3, 6 hrs post‑cath to document any procedural injury. | Immediate | | **Infection Prophylaxis** | Given immunosuppressive regimen, maintain strict aseptic technique; monitor CBC/CRP for early detection of infection. | Throughout stay | | **Counseling on Cannabis Use** | Discuss potential cardiovascular effects; advise limiting edibles especially around procedure time. | At next visit | | **Follow‑up Cardiology Appointment** | Schedule outpatient review within 30 days post‑angiogram to assess findings, adjust HF therapy, and plan long‑term management. | 30 d | --- ### Summary James Igoe is a 66‑year‑old male with significant cardiac history (reduced EF, LBBB) and severe COPD who has recently experienced exertional chest pain leading to an admission for suspected ACS. His work‑up shows mild troponin elevation likely due to type‑II mechanism, non‑diagnostic stress test, and echo confirming moderate LV dysfunction. He will undergo coronary angiography (“dye” study) shortly. The cardiology team recommends resuming beta‑blockade, restarting statin therapy, maintaining dual antiplatelet coverage, optimizing ACEi/ARB and diuretics, ensuring appropriate peri‑procedural oxygenation, monitoring troponin trends, and reinforcing COPD control and cannabis counseling. A follow‑up appointment is planned within one month to reassess his cardiac status and refine the therapeutic plan based on angiographic results. ---

Transcription data has the highest priorityConflict handling: if sources disagree, do not silently resolve; state the conflict clearly and attribute each version to its dated source. Clinical reasoning and interpretation are allowed and encouraged as long as they are evidence-based and consistent with the provided data. If the transcription or the source text contains a clinician’s interpretation, diagnostic framing, or stated impression, that interpretation OVERRIDES your own. Do not escalate certainty beyond the evidence; match language strength to evidence strength (e.g., "suggestive of", "concerning for", "most consistent with") unless a diagnosis is explicitly stated or defining features are clearly documented. Physical exam: include exam findings performed in the current encounter or in the transcription; never infer, or carry forward prior findings or invent findings. If the exam is explicitly deferred, write "Physical exam: deferred". If the encounter is telephone or virtual with no exam, write "Physical exam was deferred". Vitals: include vitals of the current encounter or transcription; do not infer, estimate, or carry forward prior vitals. Allergies: list exactly as documented; if not present, write "Allergies: not documented"; never assume no known drug allergies. Medications: the most recent dated medication list in the input is the authoritative current list. Spot and fix spelling mistakes in medication names using an internal drug database, but do not invent medications or doses. Do not invent drugs, doses, routes, or frequencies; use generic names only. You are allowed to correct misspellings if the intended drug is clear. You are allowed to correct obvious dose-unit mismatches (e.g., mg vs mcg) if the intended dose is clear. Format each medication as "Drug dose unit route frequency" (spell out route and frequency), one per line. If a medication is mentioned but cannot be safely reconciled with the authoritative list, do not guess; list it and then flag it as "Medication uncertainties:" with "[NEED MORE INFO: exact drug name/dose]". Devices, implants, and proper nouns: for prosthetic valves, pacemakers, stents, implants, manufacturers, model numbers, and sizes, preserve exact wording from the source. Do not paraphrase or substitute similar-sounding names; if unclear, flag "[VERIFY: device or implant specification]" rather than guessing. Investigations: list only relevant tests with dates and numeric results and units exactly as provided. Do not include reference ranges; do not label results as high, low, or abnormal; do not interpret results in the Investigations section. Interpretation and severity emphasis belong in the Impression section. You may interpret and synthesize evidence as long as it is evidence-based and consistent with the data. Numeric plausibility safeguard: do not correct values, but if a value-unit pair appears physiologically implausible, flag "[VERIFY: implausible value or unit – check source]". False reassurance guard: do not state definitive exclusions such as "ruled out" or "no evidence of" unless the source uses equivalent definitive language. If a negative test does not definitively exclude an important diagnosis and this limitation is clinically relevant, acknowledge the limitation cautiously in the Impression. Impression requirements: begin with the main problem/problems or diagnosis/cause of referral, followed by the other active problems in descending clinical importance. I f a problem is already managed by another provider, note that in the impression. Avoid long equal-weight lists; emphasize critical red flags and high-risk findings using appropriate clinical language derived from the data. Plan authority: if the current encounter includes an explicit clinician plan or next step, it must be reflected as the primary Plan and must not be overridden by older plans or generic best practice. Before finalizing the Plan, check for internal conflicts; if mutually incompatible recommendations exist, flag "[CONFLICT IN PLAN: ...]" rather than listing both. Urgency wording: specify timing (emergent, urgent, routine) only if explicitly stated in the source or if a time-critical syndrome is clearly supported by current-encounter data. Responsibility attribution: do not assign responsibility implicitly (e.g., "we will monitor"); only assign responsibility if explicitly stated, otherwise use neutral phrasing. Output formatting: plain text only; no markdown, tables, or separators. Only use plain ASCII characters. Do not use any Unicode, emojis, arrows, superscript, subscript, or other special symbols. Express everything with standard ASCII punctuation and letters.Section titles must be simple words ending with a colon on their own line. Bullets are allowed; use numerals, not words. DO NOT USE TABLES OR ANY TABULAR FORMATTING. USE PLAIN TEXT PARAGRAPHS ONLY. No tables or markdown.Vitals format if present: BP 132/60 mmHg, HR 75 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 36.8 C, SpO2 98% RA. Do not include anything outside those markers in the final note.

Exclude chronic conditions unless they directly affect today’s assessment, risk stratification, medication choice, or disposition. Do not copy forward long chronic problem lists from old notes. Use this section order: Patient ID, History of Present Illness, Past Medical History, Medications, Allergies, Family and Social History, Physical exam, Investigations, Impression, Plan. Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.

Patient ID:
James Aaron Igoe, DOB 27 Jan 1959, Sex Male

History of Present Illness:
The patient reports an episode of substernal chest pain occurring approximately two weeks before this visit while attending his regular primary care appointment. During that encounter, vital signs were taken – first reading 141/93 mmHg followed by 130/68 mmHg – which raised concern for blood‑pressure instability. He was subsequently referred to Yarmouth Regional Hospital where he underwent a series of investigations including a stress test, transthoracic echocardiography, laboratory work‑ups and electrocardiography. He was admitted for four days, received fondaparinux, dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) and sublingual nitroglycerine spray. The chest pain resolved following discharge and he has been using the nitro spray only sporadically thereafter.  

Since discharge the patient notes intermittent episodes of chest tightness that are brief (<2 min), often triggered by light activity such as walking down the hallway. These episodes have not required nitro spray and resolve spontaneously. He denies any radiation, nausea, diaphoresis or vomiting. Current symptomatology is otherwise unchanged. He expresses anxiety regarding the timing of a planned “dye” (coronary angiography) procedure expected shortly after Christmas; Dr. Moses has advised him to contact the cardiology office directly for scheduling details.

Past Medical History
• Severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) – FEV₁ ≈29 % predicted, DLCO 64 %
• Congestive heart failure with reduced ejection fraction – most recent TTE 12/17/2025 showed EF ≈46 % with segmental LV dysfunction; prior EF 29 % in Feb 2025, improving to ~55 % July 2025
• Left bundle‑branch block noted on multiple ECGs (12/16/25, 07/14/25)
• Hypertension – variable readings documented by PCP
• Hyperlipidaemia – managed with rosuvastatin
• No known history of diabetes mellitus
• Prior dermatomyositis – status unclear, likely under immunosuppressive therapy per medication list
• Chronic kidney function preserved (creatinine 80–85 µmol/L)

Medications (most recent dated list, 21 Dec 2025 unless otherwise specified):
Clopidogrel 75 mg oral once daily  
Zopiclone 5 mg oral at bedtime  
Rhonitrol® spray 400 mcg/translational every 5 minutes up to three times as needed  
Rosuvastatin 20 mg oral after supper  
Bisoprolol 5 mg oral once daily  
Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg inhalation twice daily (PRN)  
Nuclara injection 100 mg SC every 4 weeks  
Statex 5 mg oral half tablet twice daily  
Apo prednisone 5 mg oral eight tablets once daily for 5 days (as part of COPD action plan)  
Apodoxy 100 mg oral twice daily for 7 days (COPD action plan)  
Bismuth? (not listed)  
Valproic acid? (no)  
Additional medications from earlier records that remain active based on dosing schedule:  
- Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD (if still prescribed)  
- Valacyclovir 1000 mg QD (if still prescribed)  
- Theophylline LA 200 mg BID (if still prescribed)  
- Advair 250/25 mcg inhaler QD (if still prescribed)  
- Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD (if still prescribed)  
- Mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg BID (if still prescribed)  
- Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID (if still prescribed)  
- Prednisone courses as needed for exacerbations  
- Mepolizumab 100 mg IV q4wks (if still prescribed)  

Allergies:
Sulfa drugs, Clindamycin. No other drug allergies reported.

Family History:
Father had severe COPD associated with heavy smoking habit. No documented premature ischemic heart disease among first‑degree relatives.

Social History:
Current smoker: none (quit >25 years ago). Occasional cannabis edibles (“marijuana gummies”) used monthly. Alcohol intake minimal – occasional beer less than one drink/month. Lives with wife who smokes but keeps her cigarettes separate. Regular use of inhalers for COPD; adheres to scheduled doses.

Physical Exam:
Deferred – virtual consult without physical examination performed today.

Investigations:

Laboratory Results (latest dates):

Electrolytes (12/18/25): Sodium 141 mmol/L, Potassium 3.9 mmol/L, Chloride 103 mmol/L  
Creatinine (12/18/25): 84 umol/L  
Glucose random (12/16/25): 7.0 mmol/L

Troponin T trend (serial 12/16/25–12/18/25): 31 → 32 → 33 → 36 → 38 ng/L (reference ≤14 ng/L)  
CBC (12/16/25): WBC 7.2 ×10⁹/L, Hemoglobin 150 g/L, Platelets 195 ×10⁹/L – all within normal limits.  
INR (12/16/25): 1.1  
VBG (12/16/25): pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 48.9 mmHg, HCO₃ 27.3 mmol/L – essentially normal.  

Imaging & Electrocardiography:

Portable chest X‑ray (12/16/25): Normal cardiac silhouette, clear lung fields, no pneumothorax.  
ECG 12‑lead (12/16/25): Sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, new LBBB, QTc 535 ms. Compared to previous ECG 07/14/25 shows persistent LBBB.  
Transthoracic echocardiogram (12/17/25): Normal LV cavity size, EF 46.2 %, mild diastolic dysfunction grade I, segmental wall motion abnormality involving septum and anterior wall, no valvular abnormalities, RV normal. Improvement compared to February 2025 study (EF 29 %).  
Stress test (12/18/25): Nondiagnostic due to preexisting LBBB; functional class III, achieved 3.6 METS before dyspnea terminated the protocol.

Interpretation:
The patient has a stable chronic cardiopulmonary condition comprising COPD and improved systolic heart failure. Recent troponin elevations are modest and may represent type II myocardial injury secondary to hypoxia or demand during exertion rather than an acute coronary syndrome, especially in light of his known conduction disorder and lack of ongoing anginal symptoms. However, given the history of chest pain prompting admission and current intermittent discomfort, further evaluation by invasive angiography is warranted. His blood pressure readings have fluctuated but remain within acceptable range when monitored regularly. Current medication regimen appears appropriate for his comorbidities, though careful monitoring of beta‑blocker dose relative to pulmonary status is advised.

Plan
1. Await results of planned coronary angiography/dye study post‑Christmas; contact Dr. Moses’ office directly for scheduling details.
2. Continue antiplatelet therapy: clopidogrel 75 mg daily plus aspirin (dose not specified in list but presumed continued).
3. Maintain current HF and COPD pharmacotherapy: bisoprolol 5 mg daily, valsartan 40 mg daily, rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly, salbutamol PRN, rhonitrol spray PRN, statex, apodoxy per action plan, spiriva, advair if still prescribed, hydroxychloroquine, mycophenolate, mepolizumab q4wk, prednisone as indicated.
4. Educate patient on nitroglycerin usage: sublingual spray under tongue, wait five minutes, repeat up to three times only if pain persists; do not self‑administer beyond that limit.
5. Monitor BP at home twice daily; report any readings ≥140/90 mmHg or <110/60 mmHg promptly.
6. Reinforce adherence to inhaled therapies and encourage gradual exercise program once symptom control improves.
7. Discuss potential adjustment of bisoprolol dosage should future pulmonary function tests indicate worsening bronchospasm; consider switching to cardioselective agent if necessary.
8. Ensure vaccinations updated (influenza, COVID‑19 booster, pneumococcal).
9. Schedule follow‑up visit after completion of cardiac catheterization and review of findings; anticipate next appointment in 4–6 weeks unless earlier needed.
10. If chest pain recurs acutely or becomes more frequent/intense, advise immediate presentation to emergency department.

No conflicts identified between current recommendations and prior plans.

His chest pain resolved weeks ago after ECG evaluation showing pulmonary disease pattern changes. Recent echocardiogram shows improved cardiac function (EF 60%). He denies weight change, nausea, loss of appetite, hemoptysis, sinusitis, or reflux symptoms. He experienced one exacerbation in the last six months requiring rescue inhaler use twothree times daily. He was previously hospitalized postaspiration requiring intubation. He notes low blood pressure recently, being managed by adjustments to his cardiac medications. He uses cannabis edibles occasionally.
Past Medical History: Severe COPD, Dermatomyositis, Tachycardia. Surgical history includes cyst excision, left shoulder surgery, and multiple knee arthroscopies/meniscectomy.
Medications: Aurovalsartan 40 mg QD, Valacyclovir HCl 1000 mg QD, Theophylline LA 200 mg BID, Advair 25025 mcg inhaler QD, Bisoprolol 5mg QD, Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg QID, Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg QD, Mycophenolate 500 mg BID, Spiriva Respimat 2.5 mcg BID, Prednisone (currently taking), Mepolizumab 100 mg vial (EA). Allergies: Sulfa drugs, unknown others.

Investigations: ECG performed July 16, 2025 showed sinus rhythm with pulmonary disease pattern; repeat ECG compared favorably without ST depression or anterior wave inversion noted previously. Echocardiogram revealed EF of 60%. Iron studies and swallow assessment have been requested but not yet completed.
Impression: This patient has severe AsthmaCOPD, currently experiencing symptoms consistent with a possible respiratory infection which he is selftreating appropriately. Cardiac function appears significantly improved per recent WMS (EF 60%), warranting discontinuation of Spironolactone. His tachycardia seems controlled clinically. Concerns exist regarding potential impact of cannabis edible use on cardiovascular health given his history, although this requires further clarification and counseling. Outstanding investigations include swallow assessment. Also, he did not receive the IV iron yet.
Plan: 1. Continue current asthmaCOPD management regimen, pending resolution of acute infectious symptoms. Consider adding Doxycycline and Prednisone course for 5 days as indicated by symptoms. 2. Discontinue Spironolactone as recommended due to normalized ejection fraction. Inform primary care provider. 3. Discuss trial of combination ICS/LABA/LAMA inhaler (Trelegy Ellipta) instead of separate Advair and Spiriva inhalers, providing sample if agreeable. Monitor response. 4. Reiterate importance of avoiding triggers like smoking and potentially cannabis use given cardiac history. Counsel on risks associated with cannabis use and cardiovascular health. 5. Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.
Sincerely,
