Patient ID: James Aaron Igoe, DOB 27 Jan 1959, Sex Male  

History of Present Illness  
On 29 Dec 2025 the patient reports intermittent chest discomfort lasting one–two minutes occurring mainly during exertion. Symptoms began approximately two weeks before his routine visit with family physician Joel Phimarsenault who referred him to us because of concern about a potential cardiac event following an episode of chest pain that required emergency department assessment and four‑day inpatient stay. During that hospitalization he received anticoagulation, aspirin, and sublingual nitroglycerin which he has not used since discharge. He denies any new anginal episodes except brief, non‑radiating pressure while walking down the hallway at home. His oxygen saturation is currently 98 % RA, blood pressure readings taken by his GP ranged from 141/93 to 130/68 mmHg. He continues to take his usual COPD medications (bisoprolol, salbutamol, rhonitro spray) and has been self‑treating presumed exacerbation with prednisone and antibiotics without improvement. He expresses anxiety about future testing – a “dye” coronary angiogram is anticipated sometime after Christmas as discussed with Dr Moses.

Past Medical History  
- Severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (FEV₁ ≈30 %, FEV₁/FVC 36 %)  
- Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 29 % Feb 2025, improved to 54.6 % July 2025, 46 % 12 / 17 2025)  
- Dermatomyositis (status post treatment)  
- Colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected several years ago  
- Prior myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) documented 2024  
- Chronic kidney function preserved (creatinine ~84 µmol/L)

Medications (most recent dated list, 21 Dec 2025)  
Clopidogrel 75 mg oral once daily  
Zopiclone 5 mg oral bedtime  
Rhonitro spray 400 mcg translungual every 5 min ×3 when needed  
Rosuvastatin 20 mg oral after supper  
Bisoprolol 5 mg oral once daily  
Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg inhaler twice daily (orally?) – 2 puff q4h prn  
Nucala injection 100 mg SC every 4 weeks  
Statex 5 mg oral half tablet twice daily  
Apodrenolone 5 mg oral eight tablets once daily for 5 days (unclear dosing; noted only in pharmacy record)  

Allergies  
Sulfa drugs, Clindamycin

Family & Social History  
Father had COPD and heavy smoking history; no known premature cardiovascular disease in first‑degree relatives. Lives with wife who smokes in separate room. Uses cannabis edibles occasionally. Rare alcohol intake. Former smoker quit 8 years ago; former marijuana user quit 8 years ago.

Physical Exam  
Deferred – no examination performed during this encounter.

Investigations  
Laboratory results (collected 17 Dec 2025):  
• Sodium 142 mmol/L, Potassium 4.3 mmol/L, Chloride 105 mmol/L  
• Creatinine 83 µmol/L, eGFR 90 mL/min/1.73m²  
• Troponin T series: 31 ng/L (baseline), 32 ng/L, 33 ng/L, 36 ng/L, peak 50 ng/L (all >14 ng/L reference).  
• Complete blood count within normal limits.  
• INR 1.1.  
• Venous blood gas pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 48.9 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 27.3 mmol/L.  

Imaging / ECGs  
• Resting 12‑lead ECG 16 Dec 2025 shows sinus rhythm, left bundle branch block, QTc 535 ms.  
• Portable chest X‑ray 16 Dec 2025 unremarkable.  
• Transthoracic echocardiogram 17 Dec 2025: EF 46 % (Simpson), mild LV diastolic dysfunction Grade I, segmental wall motion abnormality involving septum and anterior wall, LBBB present.  
• Stress test 18 Dec 2025 nondiagnostic due to LBBB; functional class III, achieved 3.6 METS.  

Respiratory panel 17 Dec 2025 negative for COVID‑19, influenza A/B, RSV.

Impression  
The current presentation most consistent with a stable but potentially evolving type II myocardial injury secondary to severe COPD exacerbation and hypoxia, rather than acute plaque rupture ACS. The troponin trend shows modest rise up to 50 ng/L but remains below typical thresholds for primary ischemia; however, interpretation is complicated by persistent LBBB. No definitive evidence of unstable angina or STEMI on ECG or imaging. Patient’s symptoms are largely exertional and have resolved spontaneously between episodes. He remains clinically stable with adequate oxygenation and vital signs. Pending planned invasive coronary evaluation (“dye”) scheduled post‑Christmas per cardiology recommendation.

Plan  
1. Continue current cardiac medication regimen (clopidogrel, bisoprolol, rosuvastatin).  
2. Maintain COPD therapy (salbutamol PRN, rhonitro spray, steroids as indicated).  
3. Instruct patient to use sublingual nitroglycerin under tongue if chest pain persists beyond brief resolution, following protocol described.  
4. Monitor serial high‑sensitivity troponins until repeat values stabilize or decline; arrange follow‑up lab in one week.  
5. Coordinate with cardiology regarding timing of the planned diagnostic coronary angiography; confirm date and location.  
6. Advise avoidance of triggers (heavy physical exertion, extreme cold); encourage gradual return to activity as tolerated.  
7. Schedule outpatient review in six weeks unless earlier reassessment required due to symptom recurrence.  
8. Discuss potential discontinuation of apodrenolone after completion of prescribed course; clarify dosage schedule.  
No conflicts identified among recommendations.

Dear Paul,
Thank you very much for asking me to see this 66yearold gentleman with
regard to his chest pain and shortness of breath. As you know, James has
longstanding, severe COPD. He actually had an admission earlier this year
requiring intubation. However, he seems to be doing better and says that on a
normal day, he can walk around his home without any difficulties and does not
have shortness of breath unless he exerts himself above his normal efforts.
About a week ago, he began noticing shortness of breath just walking down the
hallway as well as any time he would get a little bit agitated. It came on
quite suddenly, and he thought he might have a COPD exacerbation. As such, he
picked up his antibiotics and steroids which he has as part of a COPD action
plan. It did not help. He went in to see his family physician today and was
redirected here. On the way in, he had chest discomfort and that remained
until receiving nitroglycerin.
Over the course of this past week, in addition to the shortness of breath, he
has been noticing a pressure in the left side of his chest with exertion on
most occasions. It does not typically radiate anywhere. There is no nausea,
vomiting or diaphoresis. It is associated with shortness of breath, as
mentioned. There has not really been any progression, and he has not had any
prolonged spells, although the spell today was a bit longer than most.
His review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
His past medical history is significant for severe COPD with an FEV1 of 29%
and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 36% and a DLCO of 64% in November 2024. He also has a
history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, with an EF of 29% in
February of this year. A wall motion study in July showed that it improved to
54.6%. He has no documented coronary artery disease, having had a
nondiagnostic stress test in 2021 and a MIBI in 2025 showing a summed stress
score of 5, but a summed difference score of 0. He also has dermatomyositis, a
colonic neuroendocrine tumour resected a few years ago and knee and shoulder
surgeries in the past.
His medications currently include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, Ventolin two puffs
q.i.d. p.r.n., mepolizumab 100 mg every four weeks, morphine 2.5 mg b.i.d.,
prednisone courses as indicated for COPD, antibiotics as indicated for COPD
(he just completed courses of these as mentioned), valsartan 40 mg daily,
mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg b.i.d., rosuvastatin which he no longer takes,
hydroxychloroquine 400 mg on Monday to Friday and 200 mg on Saturday and
Sunday, theophylline, which he no longer takes, fluticasone/salmeterol two
puffs b.i.d., pantoprazole 40 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, Spiriva
Respimat 2 puffs daily.
ALLERGIES: He is allergic to SULFA DRUGS as well as CLINDAMYCIN.
He lives with his wife. She smokes but he does not. She has a separate room to
smoke in. He quit smoking about 25 years ago but does have somewhere in the
vicinity of a 25packyear history. He also smoked marijuana extensive but
quit that 8 years ago. He occasionally takes some marijuana gummies. He does
not drink alcohol.
His father had COPD but was a heavy smoker. There is no history of premature
ischemic heart disease in firstdegree relatives.
On examination, he was not in any distress. His temperature was 36.5, pulse
rate 89, blood pressure 129/86, respiratory rate 28 with saturations of 97% on
room air. Heart sounds were faint. No extra sounds or murmurs were heard. No
bruits or rubs were noted. JVP was not elevated. Peripheral pulses were
palpable, and there was minimal peripheral edema. He had decreased breath
sounds throughout.
His blood work done in the emergency room showed reasonably normal
electrolytes. His creatinine was 70, giving him an eGFR above 90. Initial
troponin was 31. No repeat has been done yet. His CBC is unremarkable. INR was
1.1. VBG was fairly normal.
James is a 66yearold gentleman with very severe COPD who presents with chest
pain and shortness of breath. This may be related to his COPD, but we
certainly cannot rule out that this is a coronary event. A single troponin
that is mildly elevated could be a type 2 related event from COPD, but he
could have a primary ACS. At the moment, we will treat as such, so I have
ordered fondaparinux as well as dual antiplatelet therapy.
In the interim, we will keep his COPD management the same. He will be admitted
to have his troponins trended and monitor for resolution of these symptoms
that have been bothering him. We may get an echocardiogram while he is still
in the hospital, but we will wait and see how that goes.
I will admit him to family medicine hospitalist and will follow along with
him. Thank you for involving me in his care.

Patient ID:
James Aaron Igoe, DOB 27 Jan 1959, sex male

History of Present Illness:
The patient presents today with a history of recurrent chest pain that began approximately two weeks ago. During an outpatient visit he reported chest tightness; his primary care physician noted elevated blood pressures (first reading 141/93 mmHg, second 130/68 mmHg). The patient subsequently went to Yarmouth Hospital where he experienced transient chest pain lasting several minutes. At the hospital he received intravenous nitroglycerine, was started on fondaparinux and aspirin, and admitted for four days. While hospitalized, troponin levels were mildly elevated (baseline 31 ng/L on 16 Dec 25, rising to 33 ng/L on 18 Dec 25). An echocardiogram on 17 Dec 25 showed an ejection fraction of 46% with segmental wall motion abnormalities and left bundle branch block. A stress test on 18 Dec 25 was non‑diagnostic. After discharge the patient has been using a nitro spray only occasionally and reports brief episodes of chest discomfort that resolve spontaneously within a minute or two. He is awaiting a planned dye angiography post‑Christmas, which Dr. Moses intends to perform in Yarmouth. His family doctor continues routine monitoring of blood pressure and oxygen saturation. The patient denies new dyspnea beyond what occurs with exertion, and reports no significant weight change, fever, or systemic symptoms.

Past Medical History:
Severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease  
Left bundle branch block  
Prior heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF improved from 29% in Feb 2025 to 54–56% by July 2025, now 46%)  
Hypertension  

Medications (most recent dated list – 21 Dec 2025):
Clopidogrel 75 mg oral once daily  
Zopiclone 5 mg oral nightly  
Rhonitro 400 mcg translingual spray 1 spray under tongue every 5 min up to 3 sprays PRN  
Rosuvastatin 20 mg oral once daily  
Bisoprolol 5 mg oral once daily  
Salbutamol HFA 100 mcg inhaler 2 puff qid prn  
Nucala 100 mg/ml injection subcutaneous every 4 weeks  

Allergies:
Sulfa drugs, clindamycin

Family and Social History:
Uses cannabis edibles occasionally; drinks alcohol rarely (a couple beers monthly); former tobacco smoker (quit ~8 years ago) with 25 pack‑year history; spouse smokes in separate room.

Physical Exam:
Physical examination was deferred.

Investigations:

Laboratory results (12 Dec 25):  
Troponin T 33 ng/L (reference <14)  
Creatinine 84 µmol/L (normal range 44–106)  
Glucose random 7.0 mmol/L (range 3.8–7.8)  
CBC: WBC 7.2 ×10⁹/L, Hb 150 g/L, Plt 195 ×10⁹/L  
INR 1.1  
VBG pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 48.9 mmHg, HCO₃ 27.3 mmol/L  

Electrocardiogram (16 Dec 25): Normal sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, left bundle branch block, QTc 535 ms.  

Echocardiogram (17 Dec 25): LV size normal, EF 46%, grade I diastolic dysfunction, segmental hypokinesis of septum and anterior wall.  

Stress Test (18 Dec 25): Non‑diagnostic due to LBBB.  

Portable Chest X‑ray (16 Dec 25): Clear lung fields, no consolidation or pneumothorax.  

Respiratory panel (17 Dec 25): Negative for COVID‑19, influenza A/B, RSV.  

Impression:
Acute coronary syndrome type II versus stable angina precipitated by chronic CAD and demand ischemia secondary to severe COPD exacerbation. Troponin elevation during admission suggests myocardial injury; serial values are trending down but remain above reference. Left bundle branch block complicates ECG interpretation. Patient remains clinically stable with intermittent mild chest discomfort managed with nitro spray as needed. Current antithrombotic therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) and beta‑blockade appear appropriate. Awaiting invasive evaluation (dye study/coronary angiography) scheduled after Christmas per referral plan.

Plan:
- Schedule cardiac catheterization/dye study at Yarmouth Regional Hospital following holiday period; coordinate transport and insurance coverage.
- Continue current medication regimen unchanged.
- Reinforce use of nitro spray when chest pain persists > few minutes; advise not to exceed three sprays.
- Monitor vital signs, especially blood pressure, during follow‑up visits.
- Follow-up clinic appointment in 6 weeks after procedure to review findings and adjust management accordingly.
- Encourage adherence to COPD inhalers and smoking cessation support if applicable.
- No changes to statin therapy pending further lipid assessment.
